title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This paper examines the "label trick," a technique that enables the parallel propagation of labels and features within graph neural networks (GNNs), a method that has notably benefited various state-of-the-art (SOTA) GNN architectures. Despite its widespread adoption, the label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed from a theoretical perspective. This paper addresses this gap by studying the label trick's underlying mechanisms. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick, achieved by applying an extreme splitting strategy. Through this simplification, the paper then formally demonstrates that the random splits inherent to the label trick can be regarded as introducing a regularization effect on self-excluded label weights. Beyond this core theoretical analysis, the paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick, aiming to: a) empower graph-based methods with trainable weights, thereby extending capabilities of traditionally parameter-free or non-end-to-end models like label propagation and Correct and Smooth (C&S), and b) mitigate the effects of randomness by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers, offering a deterministic alternative.

**Main Review:**
**(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs.**

While the paper explicitly states its goal is not to re-demonstrate the label trick's effectiveness in general GNN performance, but rather to validate its broader applications (Section 5, paragraph 1), the empirical results presented for these novel use cases are largely unconvincing. For instance, in Section 5.1, "Trainable Label Propagation," Table 1 shows that while the trainable variant slightly improves accuracy on Cora-full (67.40 ± 0.96 vs. 66.44 ± 0.93), the gain on Pubmed (83.52 ± 0.59 vs. 83.45 ± 0.63) is negligible and well within the standard deviation. Similarly, for the "Deterministic Label Trick Applied to GNNs with Linear Layers" in Section 5.2, Table 3 reveals that for models like SGC and SIGN on Cora-full, the deterministic label trick either performs comparably or even slightly worse than the baseline, with differences often falling within the reported standard deviations (e.g., SGC on Cora-full: 65.81 ± 0.69 with label trick (D) vs. 65.87 ± 0.61 without). Even for TWIRLS on Cora-full, the improvement is marginal (70.40 ± 0.71 vs. 70.36 ± 0.51). In the "Trainable Correct and Smooth" experiments (Section 5.4, Table 4), while there are some marginal improvements for Cora-full and Pubmed, the method performs comparably or slightly worse on ogbn-arxiv and ogbn-products in terms of test accuracy (e.g., MLP+Trainable C&S on ArXiv: 73.03 ± 0.18 vs. MLP+C&S: 73.05 ± 0.35). The R2 scores for node regression in Table 2 do show more significant gains for "House" and "County," which is a positive note, but for "VK" and "Avazu," the improvements are again very small or within the error margins. This pattern across multiple experiments makes it difficult to strongly conclude the "efficacy of these simple enhancements," as claimed in the abstract, and weakens the empirical validation of the broader use cases proposed in Section 4.

**(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?**

The paper highlights the label trick's prevalence in high-performing GNN pipelines, citing its adoption in numerous OGB leaderboard submissions and the KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge. While this underscores its empirical utility in competitive settings, the paper does not delve into *why* it is so widely adopted beyond a vague implication of improved performance. The statement "the top 10 results... all rely on the label trick" is an observation of correlation, not causation for performance gain, especially when these models are often complex ensembles with many components. A critical missing piece of empirical analysis is a direct ablation study on one or more of these "top-ranking submissions" from the OGB leaderboard or the KDDCUP, demonstrating the specific performance decrement when the label trick is *removed* from an otherwise identical, highly optimized pipeline. Furthermore, the paper focuses exclusively on classification accuracy as a metric for performance. Given its widespread use, the label trick might offer other significant benefits, such as faster convergence during training, increased robustness to hyperparameter choices, improved stability in large-scale or noisy graph settings, or better generalization under specific data distributions. These aspects are crucial for understanding its "widespread relevance" (Section 2.3) and could provide a more comprehensive justification for its popularity. The current empirical section, while exploring new applications, does not address these fundamental questions regarding the label trick's established success in complex GNN architectures.

**Summary Of The Review:**
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis, particularly the derivation of its deterministic objective and regularization effects presented in Theorem 1 and Corollary 1, as well as the insights provided in Remarks 1-5. This theoretical foundation is a significant contribution, providing much-needed clarity to a widely used heuristic. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak in their empirical validation, showing only marginal or inconsistent benefits over baselines. Additionally, the empirical analysis on the role of the label trick in general GNN training, particularly concerning its widespread adoption in SOTA models, is incomplete and lacks direct ablation studies or exploration of other potential desirable effects beyond accuracy.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. This technique, which has demonstrated significant practical success in numerous GNN architectures and benchmarks, including being foundational to many top-ranking submissions on the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) leaderboard and the KDD CUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC (as highlighted in the introduction), previously lacked a strong theoretical foundation. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick, which they term the "self-excluded simplification" in Section 3.2, and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. This derivation reveals that the stochastic label trick can be reduced to an interpretable, deterministic training objective composed of a data-fitting term that naturally resolves potential label leakage issues and maintains consistent predictions, and a graph-dependent regularization factor that adapts to graph size and connectivity. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios, such as enabling trainable versions of classic graph-based methods like label propagation and Correct and Smooth (C&S), and deterministic applications to GNNs with linear propagation layers.

**Main Review:**
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The paper meticulously defines the label trick in Definition 1, providing a clear and precise foundation for its subsequent analysis. The theoretical justification is particularly strong, addressing non-trivial open questions regarding the label trick's statistical properties and underlying operational behavior that have persisted despite its widespread adoption. Key contributions include Theorem 1 and Corollary 1, which elegantly reduce the stochastic label trick to a deterministic objective function in the linear case with MSE loss, clarifying its dual role as a data-fitting and regularization mechanism. Theorem 2 extends this understanding to categorical cross-entropy, a more common loss for node classification, by providing a valuable upper bound, while Theorem 3 offers essential insights into the behavior of nonlinear GNN models at the extremes of the splitting probability α.

The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression (using MSE loss in Theorem 1 and Corollary 1) and classification cases (using categorical cross-entropy in Theorem 2). The derived objective in Equation 9, explicitly showing the data-fitting term and the graph-dependent regularization factor, is a significant theoretical achievement. This formulation clearly demonstrates how the label trick inherently resolves potential label leakage issues by blocking direct pathways between input and output labels for the same node, and ensures consistent predictions between training and inference, as detailed in Remark 2.

The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. For instance, Remark 1 clearly explains the intuitive role of the matrix C (diag(P)) in blocking direct pathways and resolving prediction asymmetry between training and test nodes. Remark 3 provides a concise interpretation of the label trick's deterministic objective, highlighting its two key components derived from the theoretical analysis. Furthermore, Remarks 4 and 5 offer deep insights into the properties of the regularization factor, explaining how it adapts to graph structure and size (e.g., being zero for graphs with no edges and larger for fully connected graphs), and how the splitting probability α effectively controls its strength, providing valuable guidance for practical implementation and hyperparameter tuning. The paper's analysis of how the regularization effect is naturally scaled with graph size and connectivity (Remark 4) is particularly noteworthy for its implications for large-scale graph learning.

The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. The authors introduce three distinct novel applications in Section 4, demonstrating the versatility and practical impact of their theoretical insights:
1.  **Trainable Label Propagation (Section 4.1):** By leveraging the label trick, the authors transform a traditionally parameter-free algorithm into a trainable one, allowing it to learn a weight matrix W to compensate for interrelated class labels, which the original algorithm implicitly assumes to be independent. Table 1 clearly demonstrates consistent performance boosts across datasets like Cora-full, Pubmed, ArXiv, and Products, highlighting a useful enhancement for a simple yet effective baseline.
2.  **Deterministic Application to GNNs with Linear Propagation Layers (Section 4.2):** This application is particularly insightful for efficient GNN models like SGC, SIGN, and TWIRLS, which rely on linear propagation layers. The ability to precompute the self-excluded label information (P-C)Ytr and use it as a stable, deterministic feature input for training (as described by Equation 13) is a practical benefit, eliminating the randomness inherent in the stochastic label trick. Table 3 shows that this deterministic version consistently boosts performance on Pubmed and ogbn-arxiv and performs comparably on Cora-full, verifying its efficacy without the overhead of stochastic sampling.
3.  **Trainable Correct and Smooth (Section 4.3):** The label trick enables end-to-end training for C&S, resolving previous label leakage issues that prevented direct end-to-end optimization and allowing trainable weights (Wc, Ws) to further boost performance. The experimental results in Tables 2 and 4 confirm that this trainable C&S variant consistently outperforms or is comparable to the vanilla C&S, particularly showing significant improvements on node regression tasks with GBDT base models (Table 2).

The experiments are well-designed to complement the theoretical analysis, explicitly differentiating between the stochastic (S) and deterministic (D) versions of the label trick. The analysis of the effect of splitting probability α in Figure 1 for both linear models and GCNs on ogbn-arxiv provides compelling empirical backing for the theoretical predictions in Remark 5 regarding α's role as a regularization coefficient. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. The paper successfully bridges the gap between a widely adopted heuristic and a solid theoretical understanding, offering both clarity and practical advancements for the graph machine learning community. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

**Summary Of The Review:**
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. The paper provides a rigorous foundation for the widely used label trick, transforming it from a heuristic into a well-understood regularization mechanism. Novel use cases are introduced based on this theoretical grounding, such as trainable label propagation and C&S, and a deterministic variant for linear GNNs, all of which are justified by promising empirical results.

**Rating:**
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). (–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. $ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? - Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

### Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. This "label trick" has garnered widespread adoption in practice, notably achieving top-ranking submissions on the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) leaderboard, despite a lack of formal theoretical grounding. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), the authors show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods. Specifically, the paper proves that the stochastic label trick can be reduced to an interpretable, deterministic training objective composed of a data-fitting term that resolves label leakage and a regularization factor conditioned on graph structure. This theoretical foundation then motivates broader use cases for the label trick.

### Main Review:

### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.
The paper's premise, as highlighted in the introduction, points to the inherent similarity between GNNs and label propagation algorithms (LPA) in their propagation mechanisms, differing primarily in whether features or labels are smoothed. Given that both approaches aim to exploit graph structure for node prediction, exploring their synergistic combination, as the label trick does, is a highly relevant and impactful area of research. The widespread success of the label trick on benchmarks like OGB, as noted in the introduction, further underscores the practical importance of understanding and improving such hybrid methods.

(+) The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.
The manuscript clearly identifies the "label leakage" issue that arises when training with direct label inputs, leading to trivial identity mapping solutions for training nodes, as discussed in Section 3.1. The proposed self-excluded predictor, `(P - C)Y_tr W` in Equation (6) and its generalization in Equation (10), effectively addresses this by blocking the direct path from a node's input label to its output prediction. Remark 1 precisely articulates how `C = diag(P)` acts to exclude self-labels, thus resolving the asymmetry between training and testing predictions. This theoretical insight, leading to consistent predictions during training and inference as stated in Remark 2, provides a strong justification for the label trick's effectiveness.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.
The paper's abstract correctly identifies that "despite its wide-spread adoption, thus far there has been little attempt to carefully unpack exactly what statistical properties the label trick introduces into the training pipeline." The introduction further elaborates on this, stating that the label trick has been "motivated primarily as a training heuristic without a strong theoretical foundation." Providing a formal justification and theoretical understanding for such a widely adopted, yet empirically driven, technique is a valuable contribution to the field, enhancing its interpretability and guiding future improvements.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.
While the manuscript refers to "our previously proposed label trick (Wang et al., 2021)", if the work by Sun & Wu (2020) indeed introduced the core idea of using randomly masked training labels as GNN inputs, then it warrants explicit recognition as the initial proposal. The current paper cites Sun & Wu (2020) in the context of the label trick's "ubiquity" and "adoption," but not as its origin. Clarifying the historical development and attribution of this technique would improve the scholarly completeness of the paper.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.
Equation (3), `L(W) = sum_{i in D_tr} l(y_i, [P Y_tr W]_i)`, is introduced as a "deterministic node label loss" for a linear predictor `P Y_tr W` akin to SGC, but without node features. The intuition for introducing `W` here could be to allow the model to learn complex relationships between class labels or to adapt the propagation process to specific task nuances, moving beyond the fixed, parameter-free diffusion of traditional label propagation. This is partially alluded to in Section 4.1, which discusses "interrelated class labels" and the potential for "node-dependent or edge-dependent weights" to enhance performance. However, a more direct explanation in Section 3.1 for this initial formulation would be beneficial. Similarly, for the trainable C&S in Section 4.3, the introduction of `W_c` and `W_s` in Equation (14) (`f_TC&S(Y_in, Y_tilde; W) = P_s((Y_in + (M_te + M_out) Y_tilde)W_s + P_s(M_te + M_out) E_in W_cW_s)`) aims to make the correction and smoothing steps learnable. While the paper states this can "further boost performance" by equipping C&S with trainable weights, the specific functional role and intuition for these weight matrices in modifying the propagation of `Y_in` and `E_in` could be more thoroughly explored.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).
While the introduction briefly mentions Jia & Benson (2021) as an "elegant generative framework that uniﬁes LPA and GNNs," the paper does not offer a detailed comparison of its theoretical contributions (e.g., Theorem 1, Corollary 1) with such unifying models. Given the paper's emphasis on providing a theoretical foundation for the label trick and its connections to both label and feature propagation, a more in-depth discussion or comparison with other theoretical frameworks that bridge GNNs and LPAs would enrich the paper's theoretical context and highlight its unique contributions more clearly. This could involve contrasting the underlying assumptions, model architectures, and practical implications of the different theoretical perspectives.

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to $ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $. Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.
The notation `E_splits` needs clearer definition, especially concerning the distribution over which the expectation is taken. For Equation (5), the transition from the second-to-last term, `E_splits [sum_{i in D_out} l(y_i, [P Y_in W]_i)]`, to the last term, `1/|D_tr| sum_{i in D_tr} l(y_i, [(P - C)Y_tr W]_i)`, is not immediately obvious. The reviewer's intuition that `|D_out|=1` implies an expectation over a uniform choice of `i` from `D_tr`, leading to a sum `1/|D_tr| sum_{i in D_tr} ...`, aligns with the structure of the final term. A step-by-step derivation or a more explicit explanation of how `E_splits` and the condition `|D_out|=1` lead to the `(P - C)` term would significantly enhance clarity. The definition of `Y_i` as a matrix with only the `i`-th row of `Y` and zeros elsewhere, along with `C = diag(P)`, is crucial here and warrants more explicit connection to this transition.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.
The density of notation, especially with multiple subscripts in matrix definitions and expectations (e.g., `M_tr`, `Y_tr`, `P_tr`, `C_tr`, `Q_tr` in Appendix B.1), can be challenging to follow. Additionally, the "Remarks" sections (e.g., Remarks 3, 4, 5 in Section 3.3) contain important insights but are presented as a continuous stream of observations rather than being integrated into a more structured discussion. Reorganizing these remarks into distinct subsections or integrating them into the main prose with clearer headings could improve readability and the flow of the theoretical exposition.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.
The manuscript already uses R^2 values in Table 2, which is an appropriate metric for node regression tasks. However, the table caption "R2 on node regression tasks..." could be made more explicit in stating that the reported values *are* R^2, or the surrounding text in Section 5.4 could clearly confirm R^2 as the evaluation metric to prevent any potential ambiguity, especially since R^2 is typically a measure of explained variance rather than a direct accuracy percentage.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.
Section 4.1, "Trainable Label Propagation," is indeed concise and lacks sufficient detail for full understanding. It mentions adopting "the randomized label trick from Section 3.1, or its deterministic simplification from Section 3.2" to "learn a label propagation weight matrix W." However, it's not clear how the training objective (e.g., Equation 4 or 5) is applied to learn `W` for `P Y_tr W`. A more explicit description of the loss function, the role of `P` (e.g., if it's derived from the Neumann series approximation as mentioned in Section 2.1), and how `W` modifies this process would be valuable. The final architecture (e.g., if `P` is fixed and `W` is a simple linear layer, or if `W` is embedded within `P` itself) also needs clarification. Additionally, referencing existing literature on learning graph structure, adaptive adjacency matrices, or propagation parameters (beyond just node-dependent or edge-dependent weights mentioned) would provide valuable context for this novel use case.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
In Figure 1, the right plot (for the GCN model) only shows "label trick (S)" and "w/o label trick." It would be informative to also include "label trick (D)" for a complete comparison, especially given its presence in the left plot for the linear model and its theoretical motivation in the paper. Regarding the GNN used, Section C.3 clarifies that it is a "three-layer GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017)," but this detail should ideally be present in the main text (Section 5.3) for immediate context.

- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"?
Remark 4 states: "If the graph has no edges, then there is no chance for overﬁtting to labels and (diag(P^T P) - C^T C)^(1/2) = 0 shuts off the superfluous regularization." The reasoning for "no chance for overfitting to labels" in a graph with no edges needs to be expanded. Without any connections, label information cannot propagate from labeled nodes to unlabeled ones, nor can a model learn to leverage local label patterns for prediction. In this isolated scenario, the concept of "overfitting to labels" through graph structure becomes moot, as the graph structure itself provides no signal for label propagation. This explanation would clarify the intuitive link between an edgeless graph and the irrelevance of graph-based label overfitting.

- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?
Table 3 presents results using the "deterministic label trick (D)" applied to GNNs with linear layers. Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 derive that the *stochastic* label trick (S) corresponds to a deterministic objective that *includes* a graph-dependent regularization term `(1 - alpha)/alpha ||Gamma W_y||^2_F`. The question arises whether this theoretically derived regularization term is *explicitly added* to the loss function when training with the "deterministic label trick (D)" for the experiments in Table 3, or if "label trick (D)" simply refers to using the `(P - C)Y_tr` as input features (as described in Section 4.2) without an explicit regularization term in the loss. Clarifying whether and how this theoretical finding translates into the practical implementation of "label trick (D)" in the experiments would be important.

### Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5) for the loss function argument `f[X, Y_in; W]_i` vs `f(X, Y_in; W)_i`. A consistent style should be adopted throughout.

### Summary Of The Review:
The paper tackles a fundamentally important problem of theoretically grounding the widely used "label trick" in graph neural networks, offering valuable insights into its regularization effects and proposing novel applications. The motivation for addressing label leakage and providing a deterministic interpretation is strong. However, the clarity of certain theoretical derivations, particularly for Equation (5), needs improvement. More detailed explanations of the training procedures for new use cases (e.g., trainable label propagation and C&S), deeper comparisons with related theoretical works, and better presentation of notation and remarks would significantly enhance the paper's impact and readability. While the strengths are notable, the aforementioned issues collectively indicate that the paper needs further refinement and clarification to fully meet the standards of a top-tier conference.

### Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? $ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

### Summary Of The Paper:
The paper focuses on the "label trick," a novel technique for graph-based semi-supervised learning that has gained significant traction in the community. In this setting, we are presented with some labeled data points and a larger set of unlabeled data points, with the primary objective being to accurately predict the labels for these unlabeled nodes. The data points are structured as vertices within a graph, where edges signify relationships or similarities between points, potentially incorporating a weighting scheme. Traditional methods often diverge: some, like Label Propagation Algorithms (LPA), diffuse known labels across the graph using mechanisms derived from the graph Laplacian, primarily leveraging label information. Others, such as Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), learn parameters to transform node features into predictive embeddings, relying more on feature propagation and trainable weights. The authors contend that the former method primarily utilizes labels, while the latter focuses on features, often based on a graphical model. They argue that an optimal approach should simultaneously leverage both types of information, and they propose that the "label trick" effectively achieves this.

The core idea of the label trick, as formally defined in Definition 1 on page 3, is to strategically use a randomly-selected portion of the training labels (denoted as `Din`) as additional GNN inputs, concatenated with the original node features. Predictions are then made on the remaining labels (`Dout`), which serve as supervision. This dropout-like strategy, where a portion of ground-truth labels is hidden during training, prevents the model from learning a trivial identity mapping from known labels to outputs, which would lead to poor generalization. The efficacy of this technique is underscored by its widespread adoption in top-ranking submissions on benchmarks like the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) leaderboard and the KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC, as highlighted on page 2.

The paper aims to provide a theoretical foundation for the label trick by analyzing its behavior under simplifying assumptions, particularly with linear models akin to SGC. It also explores novel applications and use cases for this technique, such as enabling trainable versions of previously parameter-free or non-end-to-end graph algorithms.

### Main Review:

**Strength:**
- It is nice to take a step back to try to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the label trick can be understood with some simple models. The paper successfully reduces the stochastic label trick to an interpretable, deterministic training objective, as shown in Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 (page 5). This decomposition into a data-fitting term that resolves label leakage and a graph-dependent regularization factor provides valuable insight into the underlying mechanisms of this widely used heuristic. The analysis, particularly in Remarks 3, 4, and 5 (page 5-6), elucidates how the label trick implicitly introduces regularization and adapts to graph structure and size.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written.

**Weaknesses:**
- The results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. While the introduction briefly mentions the interrelation between GNNs and LPA, a deeper dive into *why* their parallel use is challenging or why existing methods like APPNP or C&S are not "end-to-end trainable and easily paired with arbitrary GNN architectures" (page 2) would provide stronger motivation for the label trick's unique contribution. Furthermore, the duality between the stochastic nature of the label trick and the derived deterministic regularization is not really backed up by "to-the-point" experiments. While Figure 1 (page 9) does show the effect of the splitting probability α on validation accuracy for both stochastic (`label trick (S)`) and deterministic (`label trick (D)`) versions, the experiments primarily demonstrate performance trends rather than a direct empirical validation of the theoretically derived regularization term `(1-α)/α ||ΓW_y||^2_F` from Corollary 1. A more direct empirical link showing how this specific regularization factor impacts model behavior would strengthen the theoretical claims.
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later in the 'Notes' section.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. The abstract and Section 1 explicitly state that label propagation "operates independently of the node features" and "cannot exploit node features." This statement requires careful qualification. If the graph structure itself (e.g., constructed via k-nearest neighbors based on node features) is derived from features, then label propagation *indirectly* leverages feature information, even if its propagation mechanism doesn't directly process feature values. This distinction is crucial. Also, if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNNs, I believe the graph structure will then also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is built based on feature similarity. This creates a potential circular dependency that the paper does not fully address.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick, I do not have the impression to have learned a lot from it. For example, why does the self-excluded prediction (Section 3.2), which is the deterministic analogue, not work as well as the full execution of the label trick (stochastic version)? The theoretical framework suggests the full execution introduces a regularization factor, but the practical implications of this factor in making the stochastic version superior are not deeply explored. Also, why is the label trick better than GNNs with other forms of regularization, such as a Laplacian regularization akin to Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al., or a MixUp regularization akin to Lopez-Paz et al.? The experimental section (Section 5) focuses on demonstrating the label trick's own applications and parameter sensitivity rather than providing a comprehensive comparative analysis against alternative, well-established regularization strategies in GNNs.

### Notes

**Page 1:**
1/ The readability of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and be more rigorous. For example, concerning "straight to the point," at line 4 in the introduction, the discussion about "non-iid structure among samples" and the "smoothing assumption" could be streamlined. The fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and that samples are non-i.i.d. are two distinct, albeit related, problems. Discussing i.i.d. samples only adds confusion for the reader.
For example, regarding "rigorous," in the abstract, the phrase "identity mapping" from input to output is imprecise. I believe the authors are referring to a scenario in a transductive setting where, if known labels are fed directly as input and the model is only trained to match these labels, a trivial mapping (e.g., an identity function that simply outputs its label input) can be learned, as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3) (page 4). However, an "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d (features) to outputs in R^c (labels) is not well-defined, and the statement would benefit from clarification on what specific mapping is implied to achieve minimal loss at the expense of generalization.

**Page 2:**
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example, the paper defines the adjacency matrix `A` as "(unweighted)" on page 2. This contradicts the general suggestion that `A` is the "matrix of weights." Clarifying whether `A` is binary or can represent general positive weights would be helpful, especially in the context of the symmetric normalized adjacency matrix `S = D^(-1/2) A D^(-1/2)`. While `S` remains symmetric, the implications of an unweighted `A` for the 'smoothness assumption' should be explicitly discussed.
2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels." Given that the graph is specified as unweighted, this implies a binary connectivity assumption for label sharing. Explicitly stating this fundamental assumption, which underlies many graph-based semi-supervised learning methods, would enhance clarity.
3/ In Eq.(1) (page 3), which defines the energy function for label propagation, I believe the second `F` in the first term, `(1 - λ)||F - Y_tr||^2_F`, should be `F_tr` (meaning `M_tr F`), or its implications should be thoroughly explained. As `Y_tr = M_tr Y` is zero for unlabeled (test) nodes, the term `||F - Y_tr||^2_F` effectively introduces a penalization pushing `F` towards zero on testing data. While this is a common formulation in LPA, it should be acknowledged that this implicitly biases predictions for unlabeled nodes towards zero, which might be problematic if `Y=0` is an informative label (e.g., a valid class ID in a multi-class setting, or representing a 'negative' class). The paper should specify whether `Y` encodes categorical labels (e.g., one-hot) and how `Y=0` is interpreted. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explicitly state that `F` is in `R^(n x c)`, consistent with `Y`, and to mention that minimizing this objective can often be done independently for each coordinate (class) of `Y` if there are no inter-class constraints on `F`.

**Page 3:**
1/ There is a mistake with `P ≈ PY` in the second paragraph, which appears to be a typo. The text should likely read `F* = PY`, and its approximation `PY` is often approximated in practice via `PY ≈ (1 − λ) Σ(k, i=0) λ^i S^i Y`. Furthermore, the approximation using a finite sum can be rigorously stated as an equality with an infinite sum, given that `λS` is a contracting operator (implying convergence of the geometric series `(I - λS)^(-1) = Σ(inf, i=0) (λS)^i` in any norm).
2/ The statement "where […] P smooths the training labels" is misleading because `P` (the inverse matrix `(1 - λ)(I - λS)^(-1)`) is not directly used to define the iterative update `F^(k+1) = λSF^(k) + (1 − λ)F^(0)`. Instead, `S` is the operator that smooths labels. This observation should be clarified and perhaps placed after the full definition of `P = (1 - λ)(I - λS)^(-1)` to avoid confusion about which component performs the explicit smoothing in the iterative process.

**Page 4:**
1/ The phrase "model from (…)" regarding SGC should have the parenthesis removed for better flow.
2/ A more detailed note on SGC (Simplifying Graph Convolutional Networks) would be highly appreciated, explaining its architecture (linear propagation layers) and its relevance as a simplifying assumption for theoretical analysis in this context. SGC's simplicity makes it an ideal candidate for deriving closed-form solutions, but this should be explicitly motivated. Space could potentially be found by condensing sections 3.2 and 3.3, which are quite dense with derivations.
3/ What is `P` in Eq.(3)? In Section 3.1, `P` is vaguely defined as "any reasonable propagation matrix," but its specific form in the context of the linear SGC-like predictor `[P Y_tr W]_i` is not explicitly fixed. For a linear model, `P` would typically correspond to `S^k` for some `k` (as in SGC) or the `P` from LPA. Clarifying the exact definition of `P` used in this theoretical setup is crucial for rigor.

**Appendix:**
There are several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

**References formatting:**
ArXiv preprints should be consistently formatted or distinguished from journal and conference publications.

### Summary Of The Review:
Overall, while providing justifications for practices widely adopted in the GNN community is a valuable endeavor, I think this paper falls short of providing a fully satisfactory analysis of the label trick. The theoretical explanations, while insightful for linear models, do not rigorously explain its superiority or comparative advantages against other established regularization methods in GNNs. The experimental section primarily validates the proposed use cases and parameter sensitivities rather than offering a broad comparison. I think this stream of research is valuable, but this is too early for publication in its current form.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.

Main Review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

Summary Of The Review:
Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.
