reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes to learn neural environment models they term Synthetic Environments, such that planning in the model with model-free RL results in good performance in the real environment.
The authors study learning full models as well as reward-only models they call Reward Networks, investigating the feasibility of learning these models as well as their robustness to different inner-loop algorithms and hyperparameters.
reviews.main_review:
This is an interesting line of work and initial investigation. The exposition of the method and description of experiments are quite clear and complete. However, there are a few weaknesses.

The motivation for this work deserves more attention. The authors suggest that SE’s could be used for AutoML, iterating on algorithms in expensive environments like robotics, agent or task analysis, or agent pre-training. However, I’m not sure these are particularly well aligned with the authors’ statement of the problem, implementation, or experiments.
Most applications are predicated on the relative performance of various algorithms, when trained in the SE, being correlated with performance in the true environment.
However, the problem formulation in eqn (1) describes maximising performance absolutely.
This is approximated in Alg. 1 with a generic TrainAgent function that approximates the argmax_theta. But it seems the specific TrainAgent functions chosen are highly relevant to potential use cases – it is not a generic stand-in for an argmax.
The authors certainly understand the relevance of this, as they study generalisation of SE’s to different algorithms and their hyperparameters, and even address generalisation algorithmically by varying hyperparameters during training of the SEs. However, this is introduced as a brief comment in the experiment descriptions, rather than being central to the method.
It feels that to use SE’s for the potential applications discussed, it would make more sense to reformulate the problem statement to explicitly account for different inner-loop algorithms.
Further, simply maximising the final performance of all inner-loop algorithms seems quite limited when applications involve comparing different algorithms.
It might be interesting to algorithmically address this use-case by changing the fitness function to account for the relative performance of different algorithms.
However, it would also be useful to address this empirically given the current formulation of the objective, and the authors likely already have the data to do so, at least partially: does the performance of different HPs/algorithms *in the SEs* correlate with their performance on the true environment?

It may also be possible to motivate the work from a more traditional model-based RL point of view, which might be more closely aligned to the problem statement given in the paper. A closely related work is that of Nikishin et al [1] (who use implicit differentiation to address the bi-level optimisation). NB I don’t fault the authors for not citing this, I believe it is only available as preprint. They argue that models learned only to further optimisation of a policy (as in this work's problem statement) may perform better than traditional models when capacity is limited. However, the ES-based bi-level optimisation is expensive, so it may be difficult to make a strong case for this approach.

Another weakness of the study is the simplicity of the environments used. I understand the authors may have limited computational resources, but it is difficult to know how highly to weight an empirical feasibility study carried out on such small-scale tasks. In the absence of more compelling empirical findings, it is more critical to explore the algorithmic choices and motivations.

In this vein, I did appreciate the investigation of which inductive biases might help during reward learning. Overall, I find the case for RNs is perhaps more intuitive: dense-ifying rewards once in an expensive optimisation to allow fast iteration later. Using an additive potential form even guarantees that this will not change the optimal behaviour – sadly, it seems this type of RN did not outperform the baseline much in the experiments.

In the RN case optimising for the speed of training makes sense, but I still find the chosen objective strange. Why not optimise the area-under-curve returns for the full inner-loop? Clipping at a somewhat arbitrary solution threshold seems like it would result in suboptimal RNs (this appears to have occurred in the half-cheetah experiment in the appendix).

Minor comments:
 - In the SE experiments, it would be good to show the performance of the ‘fixed’ hyperparams evaluated with the same fixed hyperparams: this would show the ‘generalisation gap’ compared to evaluating with varied hyperparms as is currently shown.
 - Overall, many figures are a bit hard to parse. Try using more smoothing or subsampling for curves. For the barplots, maybe re-arrange them so the comparable bars (i.e. measuring the same metric, ‘steps’ or ‘episodes’, are next to each other).

[1] “Control-Oriented Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Differentiation” Nikishin et al 2021

----------------------

Update: the authors have addressed many of my concerns, ran some additional useful experiments, and clarified their reasoning on several points in the overall discussion with reviewers. I still think there is a bit of a gap between the big-picture motivation for the work and the instantiation we see studied empirically; the authors are working on an empirical investigation to help make that connection. However, even in its absence, I am sufficiently positive about the paper overall now to lean towards acceptance -- I found the study interesting and creative.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained at not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation. The empirical study is fairly clear but limited by the simplicity of the domains and the restriction largely to assessing feasibility rather than giving clearer indications of how the method could be used in practice.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to learn neural environment models they term Synthetic Environments, such that planning in the model with model-free RL results in good performance in the real environment.
The authors study learning full models as well as reward-only models they call Reward Networks, investigating the feasibility of learning these models as well as their robustness to different inner-loop algorithms and hyperparameters.

Main Review:
This is an interesting line of work and initial investigation. The exposition of the method and description of experiments are quite clear and complete. However, there are a few weaknesses.

The motivation for this work deserves more attention. The authors suggest that SE’s could be used for AutoML, iterating on algorithms in expensive environments like robotics, agent or task analysis, or agent pre-training. However, I’m not sure these are particularly well aligned with the authors’ statement of the problem, implementation, or experiments.
Most applications are predicated on the relative performance of various algorithms, when trained in the SE, being correlated with performance in the true environment.
However, the problem formulation in eqn (1) describes maximising performance absolutely.
This is approximated in Alg. 1 with a generic TrainAgent function that approximates the argmax_theta. But it seems the specific TrainAgent functions chosen are highly relevant to potential use cases – it is not a generic stand-in for an argmax.
The authors certainly understand the relevance of this, as they study generalisation of SE’s to different algorithms and their hyperparameters, and even address generalisation algorithmically by varying hyperparameters during training of the SEs. However, this is introduced as a brief comment in the experiment descriptions, rather than being central to the method.
It feels that to use SE’s for the potential applications discussed, it would make more sense to reformulate the problem statement to explicitly account for different inner-loop algorithms.
Further, simply maximising the final performance of all inner-loop algorithms seems quite limited when applications involve comparing different algorithms.
It might be interesting to algorithmically address this use-case by changing the fitness function to account for the relative performance of different algorithms.
However, it would also be useful to address this empirically given the current formulation of the objective, and the authors likely already have the data to do so, at least partially: does the performance of different HPs/algorithms *in the SEs* correlate with their performance on the true environment?

It may also be possible to motivate the work from a more traditional model-based RL point of view, which might be more closely aligned to the problem statement given in the paper. A closely related work is that of Nikishin et al [1] (who use implicit differentiation to address the bi-level optimisation). NB I don’t fault the authors for not citing this, I believe it is only available as preprint. They argue that models learned only to further optimisation of a policy (as in this work's problem statement) may perform better than traditional models when capacity is limited. However, the ES-based bi-level optimisation is expensive, so it may be difficult to make a strong case for this approach.

Another weakness of the study is the simplicity of the environments used. I understand the authors may have limited computational resources, but it is difficult to know how highly to weight an empirical feasibility study carried out on such small-scale tasks. In the absence of more compelling empirical findings, it is more critical to explore the algorithmic choices and motivations.

In this vein, I did appreciate the investigation of which inductive biases might help during reward learning. Overall, I find the case for RNs is perhaps more intuitive: dense-ifying rewards once in an expensive optimisation to allow fast iteration later. Using an additive potential form even guarantees that this will not change the optimal behaviour – sadly, it seems this type of RN did not outperform the baseline much in the experiments.

In the RN case optimising for the speed of training makes sense, but I still find the chosen objective strange. Why not optimise the area-under-curve returns for the full inner-loop? Clipping at a somewhat arbitrary solution threshold seems like it would result in suboptimal RNs (this appears to have occurred in the half-cheetah experiment in the appendix).

Minor comments:
 - In the SE experiments, it would be good to show the performance of the ‘fixed’ hyperparams evaluated with the same fixed hyperparams: this would show the ‘generalisation gap’ compared to evaluating with varied hyperparms as is currently shown.
 - Overall, many figures are a bit hard to parse. Try using more smoothing or subsampling for curves. For the barplots, maybe re-arrange them so the comparable bars (i.e. measuring the same metric, ‘steps’ or ‘episodes’, are next to each other).

[1] “Control-Oriented Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Differentiation” Nikishin et al 2021

----------------------

Update: the authors have addressed many of my concerns, ran some additional useful experiments, and clarified their reasoning on several points in the overall discussion with reviewers. I still think there is a bit of a gap between the big-picture motivation for the work and the instantiation we see studied empirically; the authors are working on an empirical investigation to help make that connection. However, even in its absence, I am sufficiently positive about the paper overall now to lean towards acceptance -- I found the study interesting and creative.

Summary Of The Review:
While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained at not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation. The empirical study is fairly clear but limited by the simplicity of the domains and the restriction largely to assessing feasibility rather than giving clearer indications of how the method could be used in practice.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This is an interesting line of work and initial investigation into learning neural environment models, which the authors term Synthetic Environments (SEs), and reward-only models, called Reward Networks (RNs). The paper proposes a novel method for learning these proxy models outside current learning schemes, highlighting their potential to open exciting new avenues of research in Reinforcement Learning (RL), such as for AutoML, robotics, agent/task analysis, or agent pre-training (Section 1, Conclusion). The exposition of the method, which leverages a bi-level optimization scheme (Figure 1) with an outer loop using Evolution Strategies to train the SE/RN parameters and an inner loop training RL agents on the proxy, is quite clear and complete. The description of experiments, including detailed setups for both SEs (Section 5) and RNs (Section 6), is also well-articulated, investigating the feasibility of learning these models as well as their robustness to different inner-loop algorithms and hyperparameters.

However, despite these strengths, there are a few weaknesses that prevent a stronger recommendation.

The motivation for this work deserves more attention. The authors suggest that SEs could be used for AutoML, iterating on algorithms in expensive environments like robotics, agent or task analysis, or agent pre-training. These applications, as stated in the Introduction and Conclusion, are compelling, for instance, by offering "cheap-to-run environments for AutoML" or "efficient RL agent pre-training." However, I’m not sure these are particularly well aligned with the authors’ statement of the problem, implementation, or experiments. Most applications, especially AutoML or agent comparison, are predicated on the relative performance of various algorithms, when trained in the SE, being correlated with performance in the true environment. That is, if algorithm A performs better than algorithm B in the SE, it should also perform better in the real environment. However, the problem formulation in Eq. (1) describes maximizing performance absolutely, aiming to find `ψ*` such that *an* agent `θ*(ψ)` trained on `Esyn,ψ*` achieves the highest possible reward on `Ereal`. This objective primarily seeks an SE that enables one optimal agent, not necessarily an SE that preserves the performance ranking of multiple agents or hyperparameter settings. This is approximated in Algorithm 1 with a generic `TrainAgent` function that approximates the `argmax_theta`. But it seems the specific `TrainAgent` functions chosen (e.g., DDQN for SEs on CartPole/Acrobot, Q-Learning/TD3 for RNs on other tasks) are highly relevant to potential use cases – it is not a generic stand-in for an `argmax`. While the method is described as agent-agnostic, the actual optimization of the SE/RN is implicitly tied to the performance of the specific agent and hyperparameter configuration used in the inner loop during its training. The authors certainly understand the relevance of this, as they study generalization of SEs to different algorithms and their hyperparameters (e.g., "HPs: varied" in Figure 3), and even address generalization algorithmically by varying hyperparameters during training of the SEs. However, this is introduced as a brief comment in the experiment descriptions (Section 5), rather than being central to the method's explicit objective. It feels that to use SEs for the potential applications discussed, it would make more sense to reformulate the problem statement to explicitly account for different inner-loop algorithms or a distribution of agents. Further, simply maximizing the final performance of all inner-loop algorithms seems quite limited when applications involve comparing different algorithms, as would be the case in AutoML or for assessing algorithm robustness. It might be interesting to algorithmically address this use-case by changing the fitness function to account for the relative performance of different algorithms or hyperparameter settings, perhaps by incorporating rank correlation metrics. However, it would also be useful to address this empirically given the current formulation of the objective, and the authors likely already have the data to do so, at least partially: does the performance of different HPs/algorithms *in the SEs* correlate with their performance on the true environment? Demonstrating such a correlation would significantly strengthen the paper's claims regarding practical applications.

It may also be possible to motivate the work from a more traditional model-based RL (MBRL) point of view, which might be more closely aligned to the problem statement given in the paper. The authors discuss the relationship to MBRL in Section 2 and Appendix D.1, noting that their work does not involve planning or supervised learning for the dynamics model. A closely related work is that of Nikishin et al. [1] (who use implicit differentiation to address the bi-level optimization), whose arguments for models learned to further policy optimization performing better than traditional models when capacity is limited resonate with the authors' "task-aware" perspective presented in Appendix D.1. NB I don’t fault the authors for not citing this, I believe it is only available as preprint. However, the ES-based bi-level optimization is expensive, so it may be difficult to make a strong case for this approach. The paper itself acknowledges this limitation in Section 7, stating that "NES methods strongly depend on the number of workers and require a lot of parallel computational resources," and Appendix C provides concrete approximate timings, with HalfCheetah RN training taking around 28 hours for 50 NES outer loops, confirming the significant computational overhead.

Another weakness of the study is the simplicity of the environments used. For SEs, experiments are primarily conducted on CartPole-v0 and Acrobot-v1, while RNs are also evaluated on Cliff Walking, MountainCarContinuous-v0, and HalfCheetah-v3. I understand the authors may have limited computational resources, but it is difficult to know how highly to weight an empirical feasibility study carried out on such small-scale tasks. The authors themselves state in Section 7 that for "more complex environments, such as the HalfCheetah or Humanoid task," 16 workers were "insufficient to learn SEs able to solve them." This directly supports the concern about scalability to more challenging domains. In the absence of more compelling empirical findings on complex tasks, it is more critical to explore the algorithmic choices and motivations in greater depth for the simpler environments studied.

In this vein, I did appreciate the investigation of which inductive biases might help during reward learning. The comparison of different Reward Network variants (Table 1), such as Additive Potential, Exclusive Potential, Additive Non-Potential, and Exclusive Non-Potential RNs, provides valuable insights into effective reward shaping. Overall, I find the case for RNs is perhaps more intuitive: dense-ifying rewards once in an expensive optimization to allow fast iteration later. The qualitative study of RNs in Appendix B.2 on the Cliff Walking task, particularly Figure 11, clearly illustrates how the learned reward maps (e.g., for additive non-potential RNs) guide the agent along shorter paths and avoid cliffs, effectively "dense-ifying" the reward signal in a task-relevant manner. Using an additive potential form even guarantees that this will not change the optimal behaviour – sadly, it seems this type of RN did not outperform the baseline much in the experiments. As noted in Section 6 and illustrated in Figure 5, the "additive potential RN (r + γΦ(s′) − Φ(s)) implementing the original PBRS formulation... often does not outperform the baseline, presumably trading reliability and formal guarantees with performance."

In the RN case optimizing for the speed of training makes sense, but I still find the chosen objective strange. The objective defined in Section 4, `ntr + wsol * max(0, Csol - Cfin)`, combines the number of training steps with a penalty for not reaching a solution threshold. Why not optimize the area-under-curve (AUC) returns for the full inner-loop? Clipping at a somewhat arbitrary solution threshold seems like it would result in suboptimal RNs, as it might not incentivize performance beyond the threshold. This appears to have occurred in the HalfCheetah experiment in the appendix, where Figure 9 (and Figure 21 in Appendix B.5) shows that the "additive non-potential RN... saturates at a cumulative reward of ∼3k which corresponds to the used solved reward threshold," suggesting that the objective indeed limited further performance improvements. The authors' supplementary study in Appendix B.4 on different RN optimization objectives, including AUC, is a welcome addition that addresses this concern by showing that while AUC yields similar overall efficiency gains, the reward threshold objective can indeed lead to saturation.

Minor comments:
- In the SE experiments, it would be good to show the performance of the ‘fixed’ hyperparams evaluated with the same fixed hyperparams: this would show the ‘generalisation gap’ compared to evaluating with varied hyperparms as is currently shown (e.g., the green curves/bars in Figure 3 are evaluated with varied HPs, making it harder to discern the exact overfitting to the fixed HPs used during SE training).
- Overall, many figures are a bit hard to parse. For instance, in Figure 3 and Figure 5, the densities and performance curves, respectively, could benefit from more smoothing or subsampling for clearer visualization. For the barplots, such as those in Figure 3, maybe re-arrange them so the comparable bars (i.e. measuring the same metric, ‘steps’ or ‘episodes’, are next to each other) to facilitate easier comparison across different training settings.

Update: the authors have addressed many of my concerns, ran some additional useful experiments (e.g., the study on different RN objectives in Appendix B.4 and the comparison with supervised learning models in Appendix D.2), and clarified their reasoning on several points in the overall discussion with reviewers. I still think there is a bit of a gap between the big-picture motivation for the work and the instantiation we see studied empirically; the authors are working on an empirical investigation to help make that connection. However, even in its absence, I am sufficiently positive about the paper overall now to lean towards acceptance -- I found the study interesting and creative.

Summary Of The Review:
While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained and not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation. The empirical study is fairly clear but limited by the simplicity of the domains and the restriction largely to assessing feasibility rather than giving clearer indications of how the method could be used in practice.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper aims to learn proxy environments (synthetic environments or SEs) and reward functions (reward networks or RNs), parameterized as neural networks, such that these proxy models provide beneficial transitions to make it more sample-efficient to learn a policy for a fixed target environment (referred to as the real environment). SEs replace the observed state and rewards during training, while reward networks provide a synthetic reward that is a function of the real reward, current state, and next state. The proposed method formulates this problem as a bi-level optimization, where the inner loop consists of standard RL under the proxy model, and the outer loop consists of NES with the aim of optimizing either the performance on the true, target environment (SEs) or a the number of training steps needed to reach a certain return threshold on the real environment.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths
- This paper extends prior work on meta-learning MDP transition dynamics for improving training performance on a target environment, by introducing a method that aims to learn both the state and reward transitions. This formulation is more general than prior works.
- Experimental results are provided for multiple formulations of meta-learned MDP transition dynamics, spanning both joint state and reward networks (SEs) as well as various formulations of a reward network (potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations).
- This paper includes experiments on transferring to different agent architectures and hyperparameter settings, which seems to be a novel experimental setting for meta-learning MDP dynamics.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation for learning SEs and RNs for singleton target environments is not convincing. While there seem to be some marginal gains in sample-efficiency, it would seem these gains are largely made irrelevant due to the additional training needed to train the environment in the first place. Moreover, if SEs and RNs require first training the agent multiple times on synthetic environments, why not just copy weights or use a form of policy distillation, e.g. kickstarting (Schmitt et al, 2018), rather than train the agent again? Further, it seems the basic ICM baseline already matches or outperforms the proposed RN approaches across experimental settings, further weakening the argument for using the more computationally expensive method proposed in this work.
- Given the wide design space of possible ways of learning the state and reward transition dynamics for a target real environment, it is unclear why only these two (SE and RN) formulations were studied. Given that this is an empirical paper, the value of the findings rests largely on a clean experimental design that provides a convincing recommendation based on comprehensive experiments. The seemingly arbitrary choice for only considering the SE and RN formulation does not provide such a convincing recommendation for using either of these formulations.
- RNs are effectively an ablation of SEs, where the state is not learned. However, the current experimental design does not neatly present these results as a clean ablation. This is because the RN experiments also separately consider various kinds of potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations for the learned reward. These parameterizations are not studied in combination with SEs.
- Likewise, there is no corresponding ablation for when the reward is not learned, but only the state is learned, i.e. a "state-transition network" or perhaps a "state-augmentation network."
- It is not made clear why exactly SEs and RNs have to use different objectives when training.
- The RN objective seems strange, as it seems to be optimizing for sample efficiency rather than performance. In contrast the SE objective optimizes for performance. This seems like a significant inconsistency between the training methology for the two types of models, making it hard to compare the relative merits of SEs and RNs.
- The reward difference term of the RN objective also caps the maximum performance improvement compared to training on the real environment. The motivation for this objective design is not clear, as not taking the max between the reward difference and 0 should encourage learning RNs that lead to outperforming training on the real environment, rather than simply matching it.
- Further, it seems that there is not a consistent ranking in terms of which parameterization for the RNs improves training performance on the real environment the most, and similarly, no consistent ranking between non-transfer and transfer scenarios for each environment.
- Meanwhile, the SE results on CartPole and Acrobot would benefit from a direct comparison to the training curves for agents using the best hyperparameters, trained on the real environment. This would directly separate out the performance gains due to training on the  SE.
- The comparison to previous work such as Zheng et al, 2020 seems quite hand-wavey. Zheng et al, 2020 in particular is quite similar to this work in terms of analyzing the effect of learning an RN and also includes more comprehensive experimental results in a more complex setting requiring recurrent RNs. The novel contributions in relation to these prior works seems to be around analyzing the impact of varying agent hyperparameters on transferring to alternative agent settings and architectures during evaluation. However, these transfer results do not seem consistently strong for any one method proposed.
- Sampling agent hyperparameter settings from a diverse range seems crucial for the learned SEs and RNs to transfer to other agent configurations. The paper does not discuss how sensitive the proposed methods are to the agent hyperparameter ranges used during training.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: While this work proposes an interesting generalization of prior work on meta-learning MDP dynamics, the case made for these methods is weak, and the experimental results—especially for SEs—are uncompelling. Given the additional compute required by these methods, it seems that various forms of policy distillation or even training from scratch seem more efficient. Further, it is not clear why only certain formulations (SEs and RNs) of meta-learning neural transition dynamics were investigated, while others (like a state-only neural transition network) were not. Given these points, I recommend this paper in its current form for rejection.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper aims to learn proxy environments (synthetic environments or SEs) and reward functions (reward networks or RNs), parameterized as neural networks, such that these proxy models provide beneficial transitions to make it more sample-efficient to learn a policy for a fixed target environment (referred to as the real environment). SEs replace the observed state and rewards during training, while reward networks provide a synthetic reward that is a function of the real reward, current state, and next state. The proposed method formulates this problem as a bi-level optimization, where the inner loop consists of standard RL under the proxy model, and the outer loop consists of NES with the aim of optimizing either the performance on the true, target environment (SEs) or a the number of training steps needed to reach a certain return threshold on the real environment.

Main Review:
### Strengths
- This paper extends prior work on meta-learning MDP transition dynamics for improving training performance on a target environment, by introducing a method that aims to learn both the state and reward transitions. This formulation is more general than prior works.
- Experimental results are provided for multiple formulations of meta-learned MDP transition dynamics, spanning both joint state and reward networks (SEs) as well as various formulations of a reward network (potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations).
- This paper includes experiments on transferring to different agent architectures and hyperparameter settings, which seems to be a novel experimental setting for meta-learning MDP dynamics.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation for learning SEs and RNs for singleton target environments is not convincing. While there seem to be some marginal gains in sample-efficiency, it would seem these gains are largely made irrelevant due to the additional training needed to train the environment in the first place. Moreover, if SEs and RNs require first training the agent multiple times on synthetic environments, why not just copy weights or use a form of policy distillation, e.g. kickstarting (Schmitt et al, 2018), rather than train the agent again? Further, it seems the basic ICM baseline already matches or outperforms the proposed RN approaches across experimental settings, further weakening the argument for using the more computationally expensive method proposed in this work.
- Given the wide design space of possible ways of learning the state and reward transition dynamics for a target real environment, it is unclear why only these two (SE and RN) formulations were studied. Given that this is an empirical paper, the value of the findings rests largely on a clean experimental design that provides a convincing recommendation based on comprehensive experiments. The seemingly arbitrary choice for only considering the SE and RN formulation does not provide such a convincing recommendation for using either of these formulations.
- RNs are effectively an ablation of SEs, where the state is not learned. However, the current experimental design does not neatly present these results as a clean ablation. This is because the RN experiments also separately consider various kinds of potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations for the learned reward. These parameterizations are not studied in combination with SEs.
- Likewise, there is no corresponding ablation for when the reward is not learned, but only the state is learned, i.e. a "state-transition network" or perhaps a "state-augmentation network."
- It is not made clear why exactly SEs and RNs have to use different objectives when training.
- The RN objective seems strange, as it seems to be optimizing for sample efficiency rather than performance. In contrast the SE objective optimizes for performance. This seems like a significant inconsistency between the training methology for the two types of models, making it hard to compare the relative merits of SEs and RNs.
- The reward difference term of the RN objective also caps the maximum performance improvement compared to training on the real environment. The motivation for this objective design is not clear, as not taking the max between the reward difference and 0 should encourage learning RNs that lead to outperforming training on the real environment, rather than simply matching it.
- Further, it seems that there is not a consistent ranking in terms of which parameterization for the RNs improves training performance on the real environment the most, and similarly, no consistent ranking between non-transfer and transfer scenarios for each environment.
- Meanwhile, the SE results on CartPole and Acrobot would benefit from a direct comparison to the training curves for agents using the best hyperparameters, trained on the real environment. This would directly separate out the performance gains due to training on the  SE.
- The comparison to previous work such as Zheng et al, 2020 seems quite hand-wavey. Zheng et al, 2020 in particular is quite similar to this work in terms of analyzing the effect of learning an RN and also includes more comprehensive experimental results in a more complex setting requiring recurrent RNs. The novel contributions in relation to these prior works seems to be around analyzing the impact of varying agent hyperparameters on transferring to alternative agent settings and architectures during evaluation. However, these transfer results do not seem consistently strong for any one method proposed.
- Sampling agent hyperparameter settings from a diverse range seems crucial for the learned SEs and RNs to transfer to other agent configurations. The paper does not discuss how sensitive the proposed methods are to the agent hyperparameter ranges used during training.

Summary Of The Review:
While this work proposes an interesting generalization of prior work on meta-learning MDP dynamics, the case made for these methods is weak, and the experimental results—especially for SEs—are uncompelling. Given the additional compute required by these methods, it seems that various forms of policy distillation or even training from scratch seem more efficient. Further, it is not clear why only certain formulations (SEs and RNs) of meta-learning neural transition dynamics were investigated, while others (like a state-only neural transition network) were not. Given these points, I recommend this paper in its current form for rejection.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

### Summary Of The Paper:
This paper introduces Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Reward Networks (RNs), both implemented as neural networks, to serve as proxy environment models for training Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents. The core idea is that an agent, after being trained exclusively on these proxy models, should be able to effectively solve the corresponding real environment. SEs function as full proxies, learning both state dynamics and rewards, whereas RNs act as partial proxies, learning to augment or replace only the reward signal while utilizing the real environment's state dynamics. The authors propose a bi-level optimization scheme for evolving these SEs and RNs: an inner loop trains the RL agent on the proxy, and an outer loop, employing an evolution strategy, updates the proxy's parameters based on the agent's performance in the real environment. The empirical evaluation spans a range of RL algorithms and classic control environments, investigating the efficiency, robustness to hyperparameter variations, and transferability of these learned proxies to unseen agents. The authors claim that while learning an SE initially requires more interactions with the real environment than training agents solely on it, a learned SE can subsequently train new agents with fewer interactions, maintaining original task performance by learning informed representations that bias agents towards relevant states.

### Main Review:

### Strengths
- This paper extends prior work on meta-learning MDP transition dynamics for improving training performance on a target environment, by introducing a method that aims to learn both the state and reward transitions. This formulation is more general than prior works like model-based RL or Generative Teaching Networks, which often focus on specific aspects or different objectives. The concept of a "full proxy" (SE) that learns both state dynamics and rewards, contrasted with a "partial proxy" (RN) that focuses on rewards, offers a broader scope for learning synthetic data generators for RL.
- Experimental results are provided for multiple formulations of meta-learned MDP transition dynamics, spanning both joint state and reward networks (SEs) as well as various formulations of a reward network (potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations). Specifically, SEs are evaluated on discrete-action-space CartPole-v0 and Acrobot-v1 environments (Section 5), while RNs are tested on a broader set including Cliff Walking, CartPole, MountainCarContinuous, and HalfCheetah (Section 6), encompassing discrete and continuous state/action spaces and varying reward densities. The exploration of different reward network variants, such as Additive Potential RN and Exclusive Non-Potential RN (Table 1), provides a detailed empirical comparison of reward shaping strategies within the bi-level optimization framework.
- This paper includes experiments on transferring to different agent architectures and hyperparameter settings, which seems to be a novel experimental setting for meta-learning MDP dynamics. The authors evaluate the robustness of learned SEs by training DDQN, Dueling DDQN, and discrete TD3 agents on them (Figure 3), and similarly for RNs by testing transfer from Q-Learning to SARSA, and TD3 to PPO (Figure 5). The investigation into how well proxies generalize to unseen agents and varying agent hyperparameters (Table 2) is a valuable contribution, addressing a practical concern for the applicability of such learned models.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation for learning SEs and RNs for singleton target environments is not convincing. While there seem to be some marginal gains in sample-efficiency, it would seem these gains are largely made irrelevant due to the additional training needed to train the environment in the first place. For instance, the results in Figure 3 show that training DDQN agents on SEs for CartPole requires approximately 60% fewer steps (6818 vs. 16887) compared to training on the real environment. However, the process of learning *one* SE itself involves 200 NES outer loop iterations with 16 population members, taking 5-6 hours for CartPole (Appendix A.2). This substantial initial computational cost for learning the SE or RN proxies themselves significantly outweighs the subsequent gains in agent training efficiency, especially for a single target environment. Moreover, if SEs and RNs require first training the agent multiple times on synthetic environments, why not just copy weights or use a form of policy distillation, e.g. kickstarting (Schmitt et al, 2018), rather than train the agent again? Such alternative methods could potentially achieve similar efficiency gains with a much lower initial overhead. Further, it seems the basic ICM baseline (Pathak et al., 2017) already matches or outperforms the proposed RN approaches across experimental settings, further weakening the argument for using the more computationally expensive method proposed in this work. For example, in Figure 5 (top row, Cliff Walking), the ICM baseline (blue line) performs comparably to or even slightly better than some RN variants, and for MountainCarContinuous, it quickly reaches a high reward, making the additional complexity of learning an RN less appealing.
- Given the wide design space of possible ways of learning the state and reward transition dynamics for a target real environment, it is unclear why only these two (SE and RN) formulations were studied. The paper focuses on a full proxy (SE) and a reward-only partial proxy (RN). However, a natural counterpart to the RN, which learns only rewards, would be a "state-transition network" or "state-augmentation network" that learns only state dynamics while using the real environment's rewards. The absence of such a state-only ablation makes the empirical exploration of the design space incomplete. Given that this is an empirical paper, the value of the findings rests largely on a clean experimental design that provides a convincing recommendation based on comprehensive experiments. The seemingly arbitrary choice for only considering the SE and RN formulation does not provide such a convincing recommendation for using either of these formulations.
- RNs are effectively an ablation of SEs, where the state is not learned. However, the current experimental design does not neatly present these results as a clean ablation. This is because the RN experiments also separately consider various kinds of potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations for the learned reward, as detailed in Table 1 (e.g., Additive Potential RN, Exclusive Potential RN). These specific reward shaping parameterizations are *not* studied in combination with SEs, where both state and reward dynamics are learned. This asymmetry in the experimental design makes it difficult to isolate the impact of reward shaping choices within the full SE context, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the contributions of state versus reward learning.
- Likewise, there is no corresponding ablation for when the reward is not learned, but only the state is learned, i.e. a "state-transition network" or perhaps a "state-augmentation network." As mentioned, this omission leaves a significant gap in the exploration of the design space for learning proxy dynamics.
- It is not made clear why exactly SEs and RNs have to use different objectives when training. The SE objective (Equation 1) directly maximizes the agent's cumulative reward on the real environment (`F(θ*(ψ); Ereal)`), focusing on final performance. In contrast, the RN objective (Section 4) is `ntr + wsol * max(0, Csol - Cfin)`, which minimizes the number of training steps (`ntr`) and penalizes not reaching a solved reward threshold (`Csol`). This fundamental difference in optimization goals makes it challenging to directly compare the intrinsic merits and effectiveness of SEs versus RNs, as they are optimized for different criteria (performance vs. efficiency).
- The RN objective seems strange, as it seems to be optimizing for sample efficiency rather than performance. While efficiency is a desirable trait, framing the objective as `ntr + wsol * max(0, Csol - Cfin)` prioritizes minimizing training steps, potentially at the expense of achieving the absolute best possible performance `Cfin` on the real environment. This contrasts sharply with the SE objective's direct maximization of real environment performance, creating an inconsistency that complicates a fair comparison between the two proposed proxy types.
- The reward difference term of the RN objective also caps the maximum performance improvement compared to training on the real environment. The term `max(0, Csol - Cfin)` implies that once the agent's final cumulative reward `Cfin` meets or exceeds the solved reward threshold `Csol`, this component of the objective becomes zero. This design provides no further incentive for the RN to learn proxies that enable agents to *outperform* `Csol` significantly. The motivation for this objective design is not clear, as not taking the max between the reward difference and 0 should encourage learning RNs that lead to outperforming training on the real environment, rather than simply matching it.
- Further, it seems that there is not a consistent ranking in terms of which parameterization for the RNs improves training performance on the real environment the most, and similarly, no consistent ranking between non-transfer and transfer scenarios for each environment. Observing Figure 5, for Cliff Walking, "additive non-potential RN" performs best; for CartPole, "additive potential RN" is superior; for MountainCarContinuous, "additive non-potential RN" shows the fastest learning; and for HalfCheetah (Figure 9), "additive non-potential RN" (or "exclusive non-potential RN") is fastest. This variability means there is no single universally recommended RN variant, requiring extensive tuning and experimentation for each new environment, which reduces the practical utility of the approach.
- Meanwhile, the SE results on CartPole and Acrobot would benefit from a direct comparison to the training curves for agents using the best hyperparameters, trained on the real environment. Figure 3 presents density plots and average training steps/episodes, but not detailed learning curves. A direct comparison of the *learning trajectory* (e.g., reward vs. environmental interactions) between agents trained on SEs and agents trained directly on the real environment with optimal hyperparameters would more clearly demonstrate the claimed efficiency gains and final performance levels.
- The comparison to previous work such as Zheng et al, 2020 seems quite hand-wavey. Zheng et al, 2020 in particular is quite similar to this work in terms of analyzing the effect of learning an RN and also includes more comprehensive experimental results in a more complex setting requiring recurrent RNs. The paper's claim of novelty regarding agent-agnosticism and transferability of RNs (Section 2) is significant, but the depth of comparison to existing methods, especially those tackling similar problems, feels insufficient. The novel contributions in relation to these prior works seems to be around analyzing the impact of varying agent hyperparameters on transferring to alternative agent settings and architectures during evaluation. However, these transfer results do not seem consistently strong for any one method proposed.
- Sampling agent hyperparameter settings from a diverse range seems crucial for the learned SEs and RNs to transfer to other agent configurations. The paper does not discuss how sensitive the proposed methods are to the agent hyperparameter ranges used during training. Table 2 provides the specific ranges used for sampling, but it remains unclear if the effectiveness and transferability of the learned proxies would hold if these ranges were different or if the agent hyperparameters were outside these predefined boundaries. This lack of sensitivity analysis for the hyperparameter *ranges themselves* is a critical limitation for practical application and understanding the robustness of the method. For instance, if the chosen ranges were too narrow or poorly chosen, the "HPs: varied" case might falsely appear robust.

### Summary Of The Review:
While this work proposes an interesting generalization of prior work on meta-learning MDP dynamics through Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Reward Networks (RNs), the case made for the practical utility of these methods is weak, and the empirical results—especially for SEs—are uncompelling. The significant computational overhead involved in training the proxy models themselves largely negates the marginal sample-efficiency gains observed during subsequent agent training. This makes alternative, potentially simpler methods like policy distillation or even training from scratch appear more efficient in many scenarios. Furthermore, the experimental design suffers from inconsistencies, such as differing optimization objectives for SEs and RNs, and an incomplete exploration of the design space (e.g., lacking a state-only network ablation). The transferability results, while a novel contribution, are not consistently robust, particularly for more complex agent architectures like TD3. Given these points, and the lack of a clear, consistent advantage over baselines or simpler alternatives, I recommend this paper in its current form for rejection.

### Rating:
3: reject, not good enough
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper aims to learn proxy environments (synthetic environments or SEs) and reward functions (reward networks or RNs), parameterized as neural networks, such that these proxy models provide beneficial transitions to make it more sample-efficient to learn a policy for a fixed target environment (referred to as the real environment). SEs replace the observed state and rewards during training, while reward networks provide a synthetic reward that is a function of the real reward, current state, and next state. The proposed method formulates this problem as a bi-level optimization, where the inner loop consists of standard RL under the proxy model, and the outer loop consists of NES with the aim of optimizing either the performance on the true, target environment (SEs) or a the number of training steps needed to reach a certain return threshold on the real environment.

Main Review:
### Strengths
- This paper extends prior work on meta-learning MDP transition dynamics for improving training performance on a target environment, by introducing a method that aims to learn both the state and reward transitions. This formulation is more general than prior works.
- Experimental results are provided for multiple formulations of meta-learned MDP transition dynamics, spanning both joint state and reward networks (SEs) as well as various formulations of a reward network (potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations).
- This paper includes experiments on transferring to different agent architectures and hyperparameter settings, which seems to be a novel experimental setting for meta-learning MDP dynamics.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation for learning SEs and RNs for singleton target environments is not convincing. While there seem to be some marginal gains in sample-efficiency, it would seem these gains are largely made irrelevant due to the additional training needed to train the environment in the first place. Moreover, if SEs and RNs require first training the agent multiple times on synthetic environments, why not just copy weights or use a form of policy distillation, e.g. kickstarting (Schmitt et al, 2018), rather than train the agent again? Further, it seems the basic ICM baseline already matches or outperforms the proposed RN approaches across experimental settings, further weakening the argument for using the more computationally expensive method proposed in this work.
- Given the wide design space of possible ways of learning the state and reward transition dynamics for a target real environment, it is unclear why only these two (SE and RN) formulations were studied. Given that this is an empirical paper, the value of the findings rests largely on a clean experimental design that provides a convincing recommendation based on comprehensive experiments. The seemingly arbitrary choice for only considering the SE and RN formulation does not provide such a convincing recommendation for using either of these formulations.
- RNs are effectively an ablation of SEs, where the state is not learned. However, the current experimental design does not neatly present these results as a clean ablation. This is because the RN experiments also separately consider various kinds of potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations for the learned reward. These parameterizations are not studied in combination with SEs.
- Likewise, there is no corresponding ablation for when the reward is not learned, but only the state is learned, i.e. a "state-transition network" or perhaps a "state-augmentation network."
- It is not made clear why exactly SEs and RNs have to use different objectives when training.
- The RN objective seems strange, as it seems to be optimizing for sample efficiency rather than performance. In contrast the SE objective optimizes for performance. This seems like a significant inconsistency between the training methology for the two types of models, making it hard to compare the relative merits of SEs and RNs.
- The reward difference term of the RN objective also caps the maximum performance improvement compared to training on the real environment. The motivation for this objective design is not clear, as not taking the max between the reward difference and 0 should encourage learning RNs that lead to outperforming training on the real environment, rather than simply matching it.
- Further, it seems that there is not a consistent ranking in terms of which parameterization for the RNs improves training performance on the real environment the most, and similarly, no consistent ranking between non-transfer and transfer scenarios for each environment.
- Meanwhile, the SE results on CartPole and Acrobot would benefit from a direct comparison to the training curves for agents using the best hyperparameters, trained on the real environment. This would directly separate out the performance gains due to training on the  SE.
- The comparison to previous work such as Zheng et al, 2020 seems quite hand-wavey. Zheng et al, 2020 in particular is quite similar to this work in terms of analyzing the effect of learning an RN and also includes more comprehensive experimental results in a more complex setting requiring recurrent RNs. The novel contributions in relation to these prior works seems to be around analyzing the impact of varying agent hyperparameters on transferring to alternative agent settings and architectures during evaluation. However, these transfer results do not seem consistently strong for any one method proposed.
- Sampling agent hyperparameter settings from a diverse range seems crucial for the learned SEs and RNs to transfer to other agent configurations. The paper does not discuss how sensitive the proposed methods are to the agent hyperparameter ranges used during training.

Summary Of The Review:
While this work proposes an interesting generalization of prior work on meta-learning MDP dynamics, the case made for these methods is weak, and the experimental results—especially for SEs—are uncompelling. Given the additional compute required by these methods, it seems that various forms of policy distillation or even training from scratch seem more efficient. Further, it is not clear why only certain formulations (SEs and RNs) of meta-learning neural transition dynamics were investigated, while others (like a state-only neural transition network) were not. Given these points, I recommend this paper in its current form for rejection.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

title: FP-DETR: Detection Transformer Advanced by Fully Pre-training

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a novel transformer-based detection algorithm (FP-DETR) that could benefit from large-scale pre-training for model robustness and generalization on small datasets. FP-DETR consists of a encoder only transformer on top of a set of lightweight feature extracting layers for pre-training. It is then fine-tuned with a task-adapter that helps the model attending to object location as well as be aware of inter-object relationship for detection task. Evaluation is done on both large scale dataset COCO and relatively small domain CityScapes with ablation analysis on different system modules and design choices. The result indicates that FP-DETR presents better generalization capabilities on smaller dataset with on-part performance compared to other SOTA in general settings.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, I like the paper but I feel hesitant to accept this paper at its current status . While it is no secret that one can improve model robustness and generalizability through pre-training, I do believe that the method contributes values to the community with an architecture that is designed to better utilize imageNet classification pre-training and close the gap between classification and detection in the fine-tuning stage. However, I found the current experimental protocol is not solid enough to support some of the claimed contributions. I am happy to change my rating if the author could provide justification through discussion or additional experiments. 

Pros:

- The paper is well written and easy to read. The intuition behind the algorithm design is sufficiently discussed. The technical session is described in detail.
- I like the idea of decreasing the model discrepancy between upstream classification tasks and downstream detection tasks through an encoder only transformer. 
- The intuition to provide location attention and inter-object relationship attention is interesting
- The overall validity of the algorithm is backed by performance close to SOTA on COCO and outstanding in Cityscapes. 

Cons:
- The main concern I have in the experiment is whether we can decouple the advantage of the proposed architecture or the fact that the Transformer encoder is being pretrained when compared to other SOTA (Table 2). E.g. What’s the performance of the proposed algorithm with normal ResNet backbones? What’s the performance of Deformable Tranformer with Encoder pretrained？
- The ablation analysis on the lite version (Table 3) is not as strong as one in the base version. It is easier to improve on top of a weak performance.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper proposed a novel architecture to empower a transformer-based object detector with smoother pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm. The paper is well-written and provides interesting discussion on its intuition and new perspective on positional embeddings in this context. However, pre-training helps improve the model is not a novel theory by itself. The insufficient experimental comparison decreases the strength of the paper. I am looking forward to seeing the rebuttal and willing to change my rating accordingly.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a novel transformer-based detection algorithm (FP-DETR) that could benefit from large-scale pre-training for model robustness and generalization on small datasets. FP-DETR consists of a encoder only transformer on top of a set of lightweight feature extracting layers for pre-training. It is then fine-tuned with a task-adapter that helps the model attending to object location as well as be aware of inter-object relationship for detection task. Evaluation is done on both large scale dataset COCO and relatively small domain CityScapes with ablation analysis on different system modules and design choices. The result indicates that FP-DETR presents better generalization capabilities on smaller dataset with on-part performance compared to other SOTA in general settings.

Main Review:
Overall, I like the paper but I feel hesitant to accept this paper at its current status . While it is no secret that one can improve model robustness and generalizability through pre-training, I do believe that the method contributes values to the community with an architecture that is designed to better utilize imageNet classification pre-training and close the gap between classification and detection in the fine-tuning stage. However, I found the current experimental protocol is not solid enough to support some of the claimed contributions. I am happy to change my rating if the author could provide justification through discussion or additional experiments. 

Pros:

- The paper is well written and easy to read. The intuition behind the algorithm design is sufficiently discussed. The technical session is described in detail.
- I like the idea of decreasing the model discrepancy between upstream classification tasks and downstream detection tasks through an encoder only transformer. 
- The intuition to provide location attention and inter-object relationship attention is interesting
- The overall validity of the algorithm is backed by performance close to SOTA on COCO and outstanding in Cityscapes. 

Cons:
- The main concern I have in the experiment is whether we can decouple the advantage of the proposed architecture or the fact that the Transformer encoder is being pretrained when compared to other SOTA (Table 2). E.g. What’s the performance of the proposed algorithm with normal ResNet backbones? What’s the performance of Deformable Tranformer with Encoder pretrained？
- The ablation analysis on the lite version (Table 3) is not as strong as one in the base version. It is easier to improve on top of a weak performance.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposed a novel architecture to empower a transformer-based object detector with smoother pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm. The paper is well-written and provides interesting discussion on its intuition and new perspective on positional embeddings in this context. However, pre-training helps improve the model is not a novel theory by itself. The insufficient experimental comparison decreases the strength of the paper. I am looking forward to seeing the rebuttal and willing to change my rating accordingly.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a novel transformer-based detection algorithm, FP-DETR (Fully Pre-Trained Detection Transformer), that aims to benefit from large-scale pre-training for improved model robustness and generalization on small datasets. Unlike previous detection transformers that only pre-train their CNN backbones, FP-DETR introduces a paradigm where the entire transformer component is pre-trained. The architecture consists of an encoder-only transformer built on top of a set of lightweight multi-scale feature extracting layers during the pre-training phase. Subsequently, it is smoothly fine-tuned for the object detection task using a novel task-adapter. This adapter is designed to treat query positional embeddings as "visual prompts" to guide the model's attention to object locations and to facilitate the modeling of inter-object relationships. Evaluation is conducted on both the large-scale COCO dataset and the relatively smaller Cityscapes dataset, complemented by an ablation analysis on different system modules and design choices. The experimental results, as presented in the manuscript, indicate that FP-DETR demonstrates competitive performance on COCO and presents notably better generalization capabilities on smaller datasets like Cityscapes, along with enhanced robustness to common corruptions, compared to other state-of-the-art detection transformers.

Main Review:
Overall, I like the paper but I feel hesitant to accept this paper at its current status. While it is no secret that one can improve model robustness and generalizability through pre-training, I do believe that the method contributes values to the community with an architecture that is designed to better utilize ImageNet classification pre-training and close the gap between classification and detection in the fine-tuning stage. The problem of effectively pre-training the transformer components in detection models is a significant one, and FP-DETR offers a compelling approach to address it. However, I found the current experimental protocol is not solid enough to support some of the claimed contributions, particularly in isolating the specific advantages of the proposed architectural changes. I am happy to change my rating if the author could provide justification through discussion or additional experiments.

Pros:

*   **The paper is well written and easy to read. The intuition behind the algorithm design is sufficiently discussed. The technical session is described in detail.** The introduction clearly outlines the problem of separated pre-training in detection transformers and motivates the need for fully pre-training. The analogy between textual prompts in NLP and visual prompts in CV, illustrated in Figure 2, provides an intuitive understanding of the proposed approach. Furthermore, Section 3, "Method," provides a clear and detailed breakdown of both the pre-training and fine-tuning phases, including the specifics of the encoder-only transformer, lightweight multi-scale tokenizer, and the task adaptor.
*   **I like the idea of decreasing the model discrepancy between upstream classification tasks and downstream detection tasks through an encoder only transformer.** The authors effectively highlight the challenges of pre-training a full encoder-decoder transformer on ImageNet classification due to issues like decoder overfitting to a single class token and the absence of crucial detection-specific components in the upstream task, as explained in Section 1. By adopting an encoder-only structure during pre-training, as detailed in Section 3.2, the model leverages established practices from vision transformers and simplifies the pre-training process, which is a sensible design choice.
*   **The intuition to provide location attention and inter-object relationship attention is interesting.** This is a key aspect of FP-DETR's fine-tuning strategy. The concept of treating query positional embeddings as "visual prompts" to guide the model's focus to target areas is a novel and elegant way to bridge the pre-training and fine-tuning gap, as discussed in Section 3.3. Moreover, the proposed task adaptor, instantiated as a self-attention layer (Equation 5), effectively models inter-object relationships, which are critical for object detection but are inherently missing in standard ImageNet classification pre-training.
*   **The overall validity of the algorithm is backed by performance close to SOTA on COCO and outstanding in Cityscapes.** On the challenging COCO 2017 val set, FP-DETR-Base achieves competitive performance (43.3 AP) compared to Deformable DETR (43.8 AP) while using fewer parameters (36M vs 40M), as shown in Table 2. More impressively, the FP-DETR-Small variant (24M parameters) matches the performance of UP-DETR (41M parameters) and outperforms Deformable DETR with ResNet-18/34 backbones, indicating a better parameter-accuracy trade-off. Furthermore, FP-DETR demonstrates strong generalization to small-size datasets, with FP-DETR-Base achieving 29.6 AP on Cityscapes, significantly outperforming Deformable DETR (27.3 AP) and other detection transformers (Table 5). The reported robustness against common corruptions on COCO-C (Table 4), where FP-DETR-Base performs best on 14 out of 15 corruption types, further strengthens the claims of the benefits of full pre-training.

Cons:
*   **The main concern I have in the experiment is whether we can decouple the advantage of the proposed architecture or the fact that the Transformer encoder is being pretrained when compared to other SOTA (Table 2). E.g. What’s the performance of the proposed algorithm with normal ResNet backbones? What’s the performance of Deformable Transformer with Encoder pretrained?** While the paper introduces a lightweight multi-scale tokenizer (Appendix A.1) and demonstrates its effectiveness, a direct comparison of FP-DETR using a standard, more sophisticated backbone like ResNet-50 would help clarify whether the performance gains are predominantly from the novel pre-training strategy of the transformer encoder or also significantly influenced by the tokenizer design. More crucially, the core contribution of FP-DETR is fully pre-training the transformer. The paper states that existing detection transformers only pre-train their CNN backbones, leaving the transformer trained from scratch. To truly isolate the benefits of *pre-training the transformer encoder itself*, it would be highly informative to see results for a Deformable DETR where its transformer encoder (or at least its encoder part) has also undergone a similar pre-training regimen, rather than being trained from scratch. While Appendix A.2 explores pre-training an encoder-decoder structure (FP-EncDec) and Appendix A.3 discusses pre-training Deformable DETR's ResNet-50 backbone on ImageNet-21k, a direct experiment where the *transformer encoder component* of a standard Deformable DETR is pre-trained and then fine-tuned for detection is not explicitly presented. This specific comparison would provide a stronger baseline for evaluating the unique advantages of FP-DETR's fully pre-trained transformer encoder.
*   **The ablation analysis on the lite version (Table 3) is not as strong as one in the base version. It is easier to improve on top of a weak performance.** While the ablation study in Table 3 clearly shows the positive impact of the task adaptor on the FP-DETR-Lite model, demonstrating significant AP gains (e.g., from 32.8 AP without adaptor to 37.2 AP with shared self-attention adaptor), performing these ablations on the more capable 'Small' or 'Base' versions would provide more robust evidence. Improvements observed on a model that already has a higher baseline performance would more convincingly validate the consistent effectiveness of the task adaptor and its ability to model inter-object relationships, rather than simply boosting a less performant baseline.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposed a novel architecture to empower a transformer-based object detector with a smoother pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm, leveraging the concept of visual prompts. The paper is well-written and provides interesting discussion on its intuition and a new perspective on positional embeddings in this context. However, pre-training helps improve the model is not a novel theory by itself. The insufficient experimental comparison, particularly in thoroughly decoupling the effects of the proposed transformer pre-training strategy from other factors and demonstrating the task adapter's impact on a stronger model variant, decreases the overall strength of the paper. I am looking forward to seeing the rebuttal and willing to change my rating accordingly.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a new transformer framework for detection task by masking better use of features in pretraining phase. Inspired by ‘prompting’ in the NLP task, it treats query positional embeddings as visual prompts to better localize objects. With the similar architecture design, it mitigates the discrepancy between pretraining and finetuning stages. Task adaptor is introduced to capture inter-object relationships. The proposed framework achieves competitive performance and becomes more robust and generalizable.
reviews.main_review:
- This work introduced the ‘prompting’ to detection task. By mitigating the gap between pre-training and finetuning task, it shows a better performance on COCO dataset. It is also better on robustness and gereralization to small-size dataset. Authors conducted ablation studies to verify the different settings. 

- In Table 7, it shows the FP-EncDec with 300 tokens performs better than that with only 1 token on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks. I wonder if we also apply 300 tokens to FP-DETR on the pretraining, will performance of pretraining and downstream tasks become better as well? It is interesting to see it as it may provide more flexibility to different downstream tasks.
- In table 2, is it possible to have an ablation study in which we have FP-DETR with ResNet-50 backbone? 

- Minor: it seems there is a typo -  PT-DETR in the introduction?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Although the idea is borrowed from the NLP field, applying it to the CV field may be helpful for the community. It provides a new perspective towards the mitigate the discrepancy between pre-training and finetuning. At the current stage, I prefer to accept this work.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
