title: Meta Learning Low Rank Covariance Factors for Energy Based Deterministic Uncertainty

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The authors explore the uncertainty calibration problem in meta-learning few-shot classification, which is a relatively new topic that was seldom discussed before. 
To solve the challenges that the few samples in few-shot learning are usually insufficient for providing reliable estimation to the covariance matrices, the authors introduce meta-learning covariance matrices parametrized by a set encoder (implemented as set-transformer). And the authors further introduce scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution for improving the uncertainty calibration of the softmax classification layer.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
The overall presentation of this paper is both clear and straightforward.

The figures and equations precisely facilitate the reading. 

No noticeable typos were spotted. 

The topic that the authors selected is important and largely overlooked by the research of meta-learning and few-shot learning. The effectiveness of the proposed method is reflected by the quantitative comparisons. 

Weakness:
My major concern to this paper mainly arises from the use of an additional meta-learned set encoder to predict the task-specific covariance. 
It has been shown in previous work that some meta-learners have natural advantages in quantifying uncertainty. 

For example, relying on kernel methods, Gaussian processes based meta-learners [1,2] have demonstrated strong uncertainty calibration without leveraging any auxiliary components. 

Additionally, I might be wrong, but I'm curious that if this class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information so that an additional set encoder can be removed. 
Please refer to Eq.6 to Eq. 9 of [3] for an example. Although the motivations are different, I believe there is some shared wisdom. 

[1] Deep kernel transfer in gaussian processes for few-shot learning, NeurIPS 2020

[2] Bayesian Few-Shot Classiﬁcation with One-vs-Each Pólya-Gamma Augmented Gaussian Processes, ICLR 2021

[3] Amortized Bayesian Prototype Meta-learning: A New Probabilistic Meta-learning Approach to Few-shot Image Classification, AISTAT 2021
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall this paper attacks a very important problem of meta learning and few-shot learning. And considering the quantitative results, the proposed method does achieve better uncertainty calibration. 
I expect further discussion on justifying the necessity of adopting an additional set encoder to achieve this capability.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
The authors explore the uncertainty calibration problem in meta-learning few-shot classification, which is a relatively new topic that was seldom discussed before. Deep neural networks often exhibit miscalibration when presented with out-of-distribution (OOD) data, a challenge exacerbated in few-shot settings due to limited data for robust uncertainty estimation. The paper identifies two key limitations in existing generative classifiers for meta-learning: the sub-optimality of empirical covariance estimation with few samples and the overconfidence arising from the shift-invariance property of the softmax function, particularly in regions far from class prototypes. To solve the challenges that the few samples in few-shot learning are usually insufficient for providing reliable estimation to the covariance matrices, the authors introduce meta-learning class-specific covariance matrices. These matrices are parametrized by an attentive set encoder (implemented as a Set Transformer, `gφ`), which predicts either diagonal or diagonal-plus-low-rank factors to construct `Σc = Λc + ΦcΦc^T` (Equation 5). And the authors further introduce scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution for improving the uncertainty calibration of the softmax classification layer, ensuring predictions become more uniform with increasing energy magnitude, as illustrated in Figure 1h. Additionally, the approach incorporates bi-Lipschitz regularization (spectral normalization) on the feature extractor `fθ` to preserve meaningful distances in the latent space.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**
The overall presentation of this paper is both clear and straightforward. The logical flow from problem identification in the Introduction (Section 1), through related work (Section 2), to the detailed approach (Section 3) and experimental validation (Section 4) makes the paper easy to follow. The authors effectively articulate the motivations behind their proposed components, linking them directly to known issues in uncertainty quantification for deep learning.

The figures and equations precisely facilitate the reading. For instance, Figure 1 clearly demonstrates the improved entropy surfaces of the proposed Proto Mahalanobis models compared to baselines, especially in OOD regions. Figure 3 effectively illustrates the "shift invariant property of softmax" and its consequence for overconfident predictions. Furthermore, the mathematical formulations, such as the full predictive distribution in Equation 7 and the efficient recursive forms for covariance inversion and log-determinant in Equations 8 and 9, are presented clearly, aiding comprehension of the technical contributions. Figure 6, which visualizes the eigenvalue distributions of precision matrices, provides compelling evidence for the increased expressiveness of the meta-learned covariances.

No noticeable typos were spotted, contributing to a professional and polished manuscript.

The topic that the authors selected is important and largely overlooked by the research of meta-learning and few-shot learning. The challenge of achieving well-calibrated uncertainty in predictions, especially for out-of-distribution data, is critical for deploying deep neural networks in real-world applications where trust and reliability are paramount. The paper correctly highlights that existing methods often fall short in the meta-learning context due to data scarcity in individual tasks, making this work a timely and valuable contribution to the field.

The effectiveness of the proposed method is reflected by the quantitative comparisons. The ProtoMahalanobis models consistently demonstrate superior performance in OOD ECE across various settings, including random OOD classes (Table 1) and corrupted datasets (Figures 4 and 5) for Omniglot and MiniImageNet. For example, on Omniglot 5-way 5-shot, our (Diag) model achieves an OOD Class ECE of 33.95±0.98, significantly lower than Protonet's 68.50±0.69. Similarly, on MiniImageNet-C, ProtoMahalanobis models show improved ECE on corrupted instances while maintaining competitive in-distribution (ID) performance, as seen in Figures 4 and 5. The qualitative results on toy datasets (Figure 1) also visually confirm the ability of the proposed model to produce more sensible entropy surfaces compared to baselines.

**Weakness:**
My major concern to this paper mainly arises from the use of an additional meta-learned set encoder (`gφ`) to predict the task-specific covariance. While the paper compellingly demonstrates that empirical covariance estimation is limited in few-shot scenarios (Section 3.1, point 1) and that `gφ` produces more expressive covariances (Figure 6), the necessity of this *auxiliary* component remains a central question. The manuscript states `gφ to encode shared meta-knowledge gained through episodic training over tasks` (Section 3.2), but it is not fully clear why this meta-knowledge cannot be leveraged or integrated more directly within the main feature extractor `fθ`.

It has been shown in previous work that some meta-learners have natural advantages in quantifying uncertainty without requiring such an explicit additional component. For example, relying on kernel methods, Gaussian processes based meta-learners [1,2] have demonstrated strong uncertainty calibration without leveraging any auxiliary components. These approaches often infer uncertainty directly through the properties of their kernel functions and the inherent Bayesian framework, which might offer a more integrated solution for uncertainty quantification in meta-learning.

Additionally, I might be wrong, but I'm curious that if this class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information so that an additional set encoder can be removed. The paper's own formulation in Equation 4 shows that `gφ` takes `Zc = {˜z − µc|˜z = fθ(˜x) and ˜x ∈ Sc}` as input, which are already features from the support set centered around the class mean. The question is whether the feature extractor `fθ` itself, perhaps with different inductive biases or regularization, could be designed to yield richer, meta-learned covariance information directly or through a simpler aggregation function, rather than passing these features to a completely separate neural network (`gφ`). Please refer to Eq.6 to Eq. 9 of [3] for an example, which discusses constructing class-level statistics from sample-wise features. Although the motivations are different, I believe there is some shared wisdom in exploring alternative mechanisms for deriving expressive covariances in a meta-learning context without introducing an entirely distinct network for this purpose. Further discussion on why `gφ` is the optimal or most justified architectural choice, as opposed to enhancing the main feature extractor or using simpler aggregation strategies, would strengthen the paper.

**Summary Of The Review:**
Overall this paper attacks a very important problem of meta learning and few-shot learning by proposing a novel method for meta-learning class-specific covariance matrices and an energy-based inference procedure for improved uncertainty calibration. And considering the quantitative results, the proposed method does achieve better uncertainty calibration, outperforming several strong baselines on critical metrics like OOD ECE. The empirical evidence strongly supports the effectiveness of the ProtoMahalanobis models. I expect further discussion on justifying the necessity of adopting an additional set encoder to achieve this capability, particularly in comparison to more integrated approaches or simpler aggregation strategies, to provide a more complete conceptual understanding of the architectural choices.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors proposed methodologies to estimate the covariance matrix of embedded features under the meta-learning setting. The authors first showed that the empirical covariance matrix is inappropriate in this setting and that due to the shift invariant property of softmax function, ProtoNet could produce overly confident predictions. 

The authors instead propose to learn the covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component. The authors used scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to make the methodology more performant. 

Numerical studies are performed to evaluate the calibration performance as well as the eigenvalue distribution of several methods.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is well written and easy to follow. Some specific comments / suggestions:
1. It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
2. What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda_c to be zero?
3. Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
4. In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is well-written with clear motivations and easy to follow presentations of the methodology. The proposal is relatively novel, and could be a meaningful contribution to the meta-learning community.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

1. It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
2. What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda_c to be zero?
3. Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
4. In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well-written with clear motivations and easy to follow presentations of the methodology. The proposal is relatively novel, and could be a meaningful contribution to the meta-learning community.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors proposed methodologies to estimate the covariance matrix of embedded features under the meta-learning setting. The authors first showed that the empirical covariance matrix is inappropriate in this setting, particularly when data is limited, as it cannot effectively encode meta-knowledge shared among tasks (as illustrated in Figure 6 and discussed on page 1). They also highlight that due to the shift invariant property of the softmax function, ProtoNet and similar models could produce overly confident predictions, especially for out-of-distribution (OOD) examples (Figure 3).

The authors instead propose to meta-learn the class-specific covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component (Equation 5). This approach allows the model to learn expressive covariances even with limited support set data. The authors additionally used scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution and enforced bi-Lipschitz regularization of the neural network layers to make the methodology more performant by improving uncertainty calibration and preserving meaningful feature distances.

Numerical studies are performed to evaluate the calibration performance (using Expected Calibration Error, ECE) as well as the eigenvalue distribution of several methods on various few-shot learning benchmarks and toy datasets.

Main Review:
The paper is well written and easy to follow, presenting clear motivations and a logical progression of the proposed methodology. Some specific comments / suggestions:

1.  It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
    *   **Expansion:** The manuscript touches upon bi-Lipschitz regularization in the Introduction and Section 3.4. Further elaboration on *why* this regularization is crucial for the proposed method would strengthen the argument. Specifically, the paper mentions that bi-Lipschitz regularization prevents "feature collapse" (page 1, paragraph 2) and preserves "relative distances between data instances in the feature spaces" (page 6, Section 3.4). This preservation of semantic meaning in feature distances is directly leveraged by the Mahalanobis distance used in the proposed method to construct more expressive covariances. Expanding on how this inductive bias makes the latent features suitable for downstream tasks like uncertainty quantification would be beneficial.
    *   Regarding set-input functions, the authors correctly identify the Set Transformer as a "natural choice" for `gφ` (Section 3.2). Appendix A.4 provides a good starting point for related work, mentioning DeepSets and Set Transformer's properties like permutation invariance and equivariance. Expanding this discussion in the main body or related work to explicitly link these properties to the task of meta-learning covariance matrices would clarify the architectural choice. For instance, explaining how the Set Transformer's ability to model "pairwise interactions between elements of the input set" (page 5, Section 3.2) is inherently suitable for capturing the relationships needed to form a covariance matrix, which is by definition a matrix of pairwise covariances, would be valuable. The authors' specific modification to the Set Transformer (removing a residual connection, page 9, Section 4.1) also warrants a brief discussion on its impact and necessity.

2.  What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda\_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda\_c to be zero?
    *   **Expansion:** The authors address this in Appendix A.6, stating they use a truncated sigmoid function: `Λ = max(0.1, σ(z))`. This is a practical solution. It ensures that the diagonal elements are strictly positive, which is a requirement for a positive semi-definite covariance matrix, and prevents any diagonal element from becoming exactly zero, which could lead to numerical instability during inversion. The minimum value of 0.1 also acts as a floor, preventing extremely small values that might approximate zero and cause issues. Further discussion on the choice of the truncation value (0.1) and its sensitivity, or a comparison to other common positive constraints like `softplus` or `exp` functions, could provide more insight into its robustness and impact on the learned covariance structure.

3.  Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
    *   **Expansion:** The manuscript includes a comprehensive "RUNTIME ANALYSIS" in Appendix A.10, with Tables 12 and 13 providing training and evaluation iteration times for both linear and CNN variants. This is an excellent addition. The analysis clearly shows that while ProtoMahalanobis (especially diagonal and rank-1 variants) maintains competitive or slightly higher latency compared to ProtoDDU and ProtoSNGP, it is significantly slower than plain Protonet due to the added computations for covariance estimation. The authors correctly point out that "Latency increases as the rank goes higher due to more factors and more iterations required for inversion and log-determinant calculations" (page 24). This detailed breakdown directly addresses the concern about computational overhead and provides a quantitative understanding of the trade-off. For instance, ProtoMahalanobis-FC diag has a train iteration time of 10.33ms compared to Protonet-FC's 2.83ms, highlighting the cost of meta-learning the covariance.

4.  In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?
    *   **Expansion:** This observation appears largely correct based on the experimental results. Looking at Table 1 (OOD ECE), for Omniglot, ProtoMahalanobis models consistently outperform baselines in both 5-way 5-shot and 5-way 1-shot settings, as well as 20-way 5-shot. For MiniImageNet, the proposed method also shows superior ECE in both 5-way 1-shot and 5-way 5-shot scenarios. The only exception noted by the authors is Omniglot 20-way 1-shot, where Reptile achieves a lower ECE. However, the authors provide a critical nuance for this exception: Reptile's "under-conﬁdence on ID data can lead to better conﬁdence scores on OOD data, even though the model is poorly calibrated" (page 8, Calibration Error section, Figure 4).
    *   The intuition behind the proposed method's strength in low-shot settings is central to the paper's core argument: "empirical covariance estimation is limited when there are few datapoints on hand" (page 1, Introduction). By meta-learning covariance factors through the set encoder `gφ`, the model can leverage "shared meta-knowledge gained through episodic training over tasks" (page 4, Section 3.2). This allows it to construct "expressive covariances even with limited amounts of data" (page 2, Abstract), which empirical methods cannot achieve effectively. In essence, the meta-learning framework provides a more robust and generalized way to infer class-specific covariance structures than simply calculating them from a sparse support set, which is particularly advantageous when `k` (number of shots) is small. This is further supported by Figure 6, which shows ProtoMahalanobis models exhibiting a more diverse eigenvalue distribution compared to empirical methods, indicating a richer, non-spherical representation of class distributions in feature space.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well-written with clear motivations and easy to follow presentations of the methodology. The proposal of meta-learning diagonal or low-rank covariance factors, combined with an energy-based inference procedure, is relatively novel and effectively addresses limitations of existing methods in few-shot uncertainty quantification. The detailed experimental analysis, including runtime and eigenvalue distribution studies, supports the claims. The proposed approach could be a meaningful contribution to the meta-learning community, particularly for tasks requiring robust uncertainty estimation under limited data conditions.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This submission focus on improving the performance of uncertainty quantification and OOD detection using bi-Lipschitz regularized neural networks. There are two involved challenges here: (i) we are required to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrix of the latent features. This estimation is challenging under low sample size. (ii) generative classifiers using softmax activation function suffer from shift invariance, and thus may become over-confident for OOD samples.

To resolve these issues, the paper focus on two innovations:

(i) a meta-learning model to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrices,
(ii) a modification of the inference procedure with the energy function to detect OOD samples.

The paper concludes with numerical results showing the performance of the proposed solution approach.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is empirical: the paper is well-motivated, and the paper adapts existing, popular techniques to engineer a solution to the standing problem (using quadratic discriminant analysis, modeling the covariance matrix as a sum of a diagonal matrix and a low-rank psd matrix, etc.).

On the downside, there are several theoretical questions that are left unanswered:
1. Why is the Set Transformer a good choice? On which ground can we justify that the Set Transformer can generate sensible values of Lambda and Phi to construct the covariance matrices?
2. There are a lot of methods from statistics to estimate covariance matrices from low sample size dataset (linear/nonlinear shrinkage, sparse, etc.). Why can't they be used in this application? Is it possible to show that the Set Transformer is better than these traditional approach (for any possible criteria)?
3. In page 8, why should the eigenvalue distribution be diversified? It is well known that the eigenvalue can be sensitive to small perturbations of the off-diagonal entries of the matrix. Thus, what we observed (diversity of the eigenvalues) can be just an artifact of the numerical errors from the Set transformer network. Diverse eigenvalues also imply that the condition number is large, which leads to higher numerical errors to the log-determinant term. Thus, I am not fully convinced that a diverse set of eigenvalues is of any advantage.


Minor:
- What is f(E) and min(E) after equation (10)?
- The abstract and intro focus heavily on bi-Lipschitz regularized NN, however, the exposition in section 3 (except for Section 3.4 which is 10 lines long) relies minimally on this architecture. This emphasis may be misleading in the first read.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In my opinion, the paper is a good engineering attempt to solve a specific problem. It lacks, unfortunately, concrete scientific contributions.


======== Post-rebuttal ========
The authors have provided further empirical results to justify the use of the ST to estimate the covariance matrix. I have raised my evaluation from 3 to 5.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This submission focuses on improving the performance of uncertainty quantification (UQ) and out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, particularly in few-shot meta-learning scenarios, by leveraging bi-Lipschitz regularized neural networks. The authors identify two primary challenges that hinder accurate uncertainty estimation in this context. First, there is a requirement to estimate class-conditional covariance matrices of latent features, a task that becomes particularly challenging and unreliable when only a low number of samples are available per class, as is common in few-shot learning. Existing empirical covariance estimation methods, as highlighted in the introduction and Figure 6, fail to effectively encode meta-knowledge across tasks, leading to sub-optimal covariances. Second, generative classifiers that rely on the softmax activation function often suffer from a shift invariance property, which can lead to over-confident predictions for OOD samples, as illustrated in Figure 3.

To address these critical issues, the paper proposes two main innovations. Firstly, it introduces a meta-learning model designed to efficiently estimate task-specific, class-conditional covariance matrices. This is achieved by meta-learning a function, `gφ`, which outputs either diagonal or diagonal plus low-rank factors (Σc = Λc + ΦcΦᵀc) for constructing these covariances, even with limited support set data. Secondly, the paper proposes a modification to the inference procedure by incorporating a scaled energy function (`h(E)`) to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution. This energy-based approach aims to mitigate the over-confidence problem caused by softmax's shift invariance, leading to better calibrated uncertainty scores, particularly for OOD data, as depicted qualitatively in Figure 1h.

The paper concludes by presenting numerical results that aim to demonstrate the performance advantages of the proposed solution across various tasks and datasets, including synthetic toy datasets (half-moons, concentric circles, 2D Gaussians) and benchmark few-shot learning datasets like Omniglot and MiniImageNet, evaluated on both in-distribution and corrupted/OOD data.

**Main Review:**
This submission represents a good empirical effort to tackle a pertinent problem in meta-learning and uncertainty quantification. The paper is well-motivated, clearly articulating the limitations of existing approaches such as empirical covariance estimation and the shift-invariance of softmax in the context of few-shot learning and OOD detection. The authors adapt existing, popular techniques, such as the use of Mahalanobis distance in the latent space (akin to quadratic discriminant analysis) and modeling the covariance matrix as a sum of a diagonal matrix and a low-rank positive semi-definite (PSD) matrix, to engineer a solution to the standing problem. The combination of meta-learning for covariance and energy-based inference for calibration is a practical approach to the identified challenges.

On the downside, despite its empirical strengths, there are several theoretical questions that are left unanswered, which detract from the paper's scientific contributions:

1.  **Justification for Set Transformer choice**: The paper proposes using a Set Transformer (Lee et al., 2019) as the set encoder `gφ` (Equation 4) to generate the diagonal (`Λc`) and low-rank (`Φc`) factors for the covariance matrices. While Section 3.2 mentions that `gφ` is expected to "encode shared meta-knowledge" and that a Set Transformer "models pairwise interactions between elements of the input set, an implicit requirement for covariance matrices," a deeper theoretical justification for *why* the Set Transformer is a *good* choice for generating *sensible* values of Λ and Φ, specifically for covariance construction, is missing. The argument that it models pairwise interactions is a prerequisite for covariance but does not inherently guarantee the quality or optimality of the generated factors compared to other potential architectures or even simpler statistical models. Furthermore, the implementation detail of removing the residual connection in `gφ` (Section 4.1) to prevent it from ignoring inputs, while an effective engineering fix, further highlights the empirical rather than theoretically-driven nature of this component's design.

2.  **Comparison to traditional covariance estimation methods**: The paper acknowledges the limitations of empirical covariance estimation, especially with low sample sizes, which is a well-known problem in statistics. However, there are numerous established methods from statistics designed to estimate covariance matrices from low-sample size datasets, such as linear/nonlinear shrinkage estimators, sparse covariance estimation, or robust covariance methods. The manuscript does not discuss why these traditional statistical approaches, which explicitly address the challenges of low-sample covariance estimation, cannot be or are not used in this application. More importantly, there is no attempt to theoretically or empirically demonstrate that the meta-learned Set Transformer approach is superior to these traditional methods, under any relevant statistical or performance criteria. This omission leaves a significant gap in understanding the novelty and fundamental advantage of the proposed meta-learning approach over existing, well-understood techniques for covariance estimation under data scarcity.

3.  **Advantage of diversified eigenvalue distribution**: In Section 4, specifically with reference to Figure 6, the paper claims that ProtoMahalanobis models exhibit a "more diverse range of eigenvalues" compared to empirically calculated covariances, leading to "non-trivial ellipsoid distributions." While a non-spherical distribution can be more expressive, the claim that a "diverse distribution of eigenvalues" is inherently advantageous requires further scrutiny. It is well known that eigenvalues can be highly sensitive to small perturbations of the off-diagonal entries of a matrix, especially for ill-conditioned matrices. Thus, the observed diversity could potentially be an artifact of numerical errors or the specific learning dynamics of the Set Transformer network, rather than a robustly learned property. Moreover, a highly diverse set of eigenvalues implies a large condition number for the covariance matrix, which can lead to higher numerical errors when calculating its inverse (`Σ⁻¹`) and log-determinant (`log|Σ|`), both of which are critical components of the Mahalanobis distance and the overall predictive distribution (Equation 7). Although the paper uses the Sherman-Morrison formula (Equations 8 and 9) for efficient inversion, numerical stability remains a concern for ill-conditioned matrices. The post-rebuttal empirical results (Table 14) showing that perturbing the learned eigenvalues decreases performance indeed suggest that the learned precision matrix is meaningful. However, this still doesn't fully address the fundamental advantage of *high* diversity or the numerical stability implications of potentially large condition numbers from a theoretical standpoint, nor does it compare against methods that might learn a *well-conditioned* but still non-spherical covariance.

**Minor:**
*   **Unclear notation for energy function**: After Equation (10), the definition of `h(E)` is given as `max(ε, -1/T log Σc' exp(-Ec'))`. However, the subsequent intuitive explanation states that `h(E)` is "dominated by minc(|Ec|)" and "acting as a soft approximation to the minimum energy magnitude (shortest Mahalanobis distance)". The precise connection and consistent definition between the explicit formula for `h(E)` and the intuitive explanation involving `minc(|Ec|)` could be made clearer to avoid confusion.
*   **Emphasis on bi-Lipschitz regularization**: The abstract and introduction heavily emphasize the role of bi-Lipschitz regularization in preventing feature collapse and aiding uncertainty calibration. While Section 3.4 (Spectral Normalization) details its implementation, the core methodological exposition in Section 3 (Approach), particularly the meta-learning of covariances and the energy-based inference, relies minimally on this architectural constraint. This strong initial emphasis, followed by a relatively brief dedicated section, may be misleading to a reader expecting the bi-Lipschitz regularization to be more deeply integrated into the novel aspects of the proposed meta-learning and inference procedures. It appears more as an enabling precondition than a central innovation of the paper.

**Summary Of The Review:**
In my opinion, the paper is a good engineering attempt to solve a specific and relevant problem in few-shot uncertainty quantification and OOD detection. The proposed combination of meta-learning for class-conditional covariance matrices and energy-based inference for calibrated softmax scores is clever and yields strong empirical results, as demonstrated by the improved ECE scores on corrupted Omniglot and MiniImageNet datasets (Figures 4, 5) and better OOD Class ECE (Table 1) compared to several baselines. The authors have effectively adapted and integrated several advanced techniques. However, it lacks, unfortunately, concrete scientific contributions in terms of novel theoretical insights or rigorous comparisons against established statistical methods for covariance estimation under data scarcity. The questions regarding the theoretical justification for the Set Transformer, the absence of comparison to alternative statistical covariance estimators, and the full implications of highly diverse eigenvalues remain largely unaddressed from a fundamental scientific perspective.

**Post-rebuttal:**
The authors have provided further empirical results, specifically in Table 14, to justify the use of the Set Transformer in estimating the covariance matrix by showing that perturbing the learned eigenvalues consistently degrades performance. This empirically validates that the learned precision matrices are not arbitrary and contribute meaningfully to the model's performance. While this is a valuable empirical addition, it still does not fully address the deeper theoretical questions regarding the *optimal* choice of `gφ` compared to robust statistical methods, or a more thorough theoretical analysis of the implications of highly diverse eigenvalues on numerical stability beyond showing that the learned values are better than random perturbations. The paper remains a strong engineering contribution but falls short on fundamental theoretical advancements.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: ZeroFL: Efficient On-Device Training for  Federated Learning with Local Sparsity

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach each node uses only the top-K weights for computations in the forward and backward pass of training (and only top-K activations for computations in the backward pass). The authors empirically show that adding sparsity in this way, while speeding up local training, significantly degrades the accuracy when directly used in federated learning. To mitigate this issue the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation since their empirical study suggests that the accuracy drop may be due to dilution of weights that are active in only a few nodes. Experiments on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model show some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed schemes.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.
reviews.correctness: 2: Several of the paper’s claims are incorrect or not well-supported.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.

Summary Of The Review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach, each node utilizes a sparse training framework, specifically SWAT (Sparse Weights and Activations Training), where only the top-K weights are used for computations in the forward and backward pass of training, and only top-K activations are used for computations in the backward pass. The authors empirically show that introducing this high level of sparsity (e.g., 90% or 95% as explored in Section 4.1), while potentially speeding up local training significantly, leads to a substantial degradation in accuracy when directly used in federated learning. For instance, Fig. 1 demonstrates a notable 10% accuracy drop for FL at 95% sparsity on the CIFAR-10 dataset compared to centralized training. To mitigate this issue, the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation, leveraging their empirical study (Section 4.3) which suggests that the observed accuracy drop may be due to the dilution of important weights that are active in only a few local nodes during the global aggregation process. Experiments initially focused on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model, showing some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed ZeroFL schemes. For example, Table 1 reports up to +1.5% higher accuracy for CIFAR-10 at 95% sparsity and a 7.4x reduction in communication costs with ZeroFL.

**Main Review:**
**Strengths:**

1.  The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops). The manuscript clearly articulates the resource constraints faced by FL devices, stating that "FL nodes are orders of magnitude more constrained than traditional server-grade hardware and are often battery powered, severely limiting the sophistication of models that can be trained" (Abstract). Accelerating on-device training, which accounts for "the majority of the time required to train federated models and, the totality of the energy consumption at the client side" (Abstract), is a critical area. The observed 10% accuracy drop for FL with high sparsity in Fig. 1 underscores the inherent difficulty of integrating sparse training methods like SWAT into the FL paradigm without careful adaptation.

2.  The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach. ZeroFL, with its three distinct local sparsification strategies—Top-K-Weights, Diff on Top-K-Weights, and Top-K-Weights Diff—provides a thoughtful set of mechanisms to address the "dilution of important information" identified in Section 4.3. The empirical evidence presented in Table 1, showing accuracy improvements of up to 1.5% for CIFAR-10 at 95% sparsity and 2.3% for SpeechCommands at 90% sparsity in non-IID settings, demonstrates that these methods can indeed mitigate some of the performance degradation observed with direct sparse training in FL. Furthermore, the communication savings achieved by ZeroFL, ranging from 1.6x to 7.4x, reinforce the practical benefits of this direction.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims, especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds. The analysis in Section 4.3 and Fig. 2 and 3, which forms the core motivation for ZeroFL, specifically investigates the non-zero weight ratio and the consistency of weight locations in ResNet-18 trained on CIFAR-10. While these findings are compelling for this specific setup, the generalizability of such stable sparsity patterns across diverse data modalities (e.g., the unstructured nature of text data versus structured image data) and different model complexities (e.g., simpler MLPs or recurrent networks) is not explored. Different architectures might exhibit distinct sensitivity to pruning or different patterns of "important" weights, potentially impacting the effectiveness of ZeroFL's aggregation strategies.

2.  The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

    a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. For instance, in Table 1 for CIFAR-10 at 95% sparsity, the highest communication savings (7.4x) are achieved with a mask ratio of 0.0 (vanilla SWAT), which often yields lower accuracy than a mask ratio of 0.2 that offers less savings (1.9x). This implies a clear trade-off between communication efficiency and model performance. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1). Quantifying this trade-off with a single, composite metric would allow for a more holistic evaluation of ZeroFL's overall utility.

    b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly. While Appendix A.1 (Figure 4) does present an evaluation across a wider range of `rmask` values for CIFAR-10, the discussion in the main text (Section 6) primarily focuses on `rmask = {0.0, 0.1, 0.2}`. A more comprehensive analysis or a dedicated figure in the main paper that illustrates the full performance curve across a broader spectrum of mask ratios would strengthen the argument for an "optimal interval level" and provide clearer guidance on parameter selection.

    c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well. Although Fig. 1 shows baseline SWAT performance for both IID (α=1000) and non-IID (α=1) settings, highlighting that sparsity causes accuracy drops in both, the detailed results for ZeroFL (Table 1) focus primarily on non-IID settings for bolding the best strategy. A more explicit and detailed comparison of ZeroFL's performance improvements for IID data distributions, similar to the depth provided for non-IID, would enhance the completeness of the empirical evaluation and demonstrate the breadth of ZeroFL's applicability.

    d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance. Expanding the experimental validation to include datasets from other domains, such as natural language processing, or different vision datasets with varying complexities, along with models like LSTMs or smaller CNNs, would significantly bolster the confidence in ZeroFL's general efficacy and the underlying hypotheses about sparsity patterns.

3.  (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. Both methods involve identifying a set of top-(1-sp+rmask) weights. In "Top-K-Weights," the client sends these selected weights directly. In "Diff on Top-K-Weights," the client sends the *difference* of these selected weights from the received global model (`wE - wt`). However, if the server aggregates these differences (`wt + Σ dk`), the net effect on the global model for the top-K positions might be very similar, especially if the non-selected weights are simply zeroed out. This subtle distinction warrants further clarification or a stronger justification for presenting them as fundamentally distinct strategies, as they appear to achieve similar objectives through slightly different calculation paths. If the resulting aggregated updates are indeed functionally equivalent, then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.

**Summary Of The Review:**
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem, addressing the critical need for efficient on-device training in federated learning through sparsity. However, given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims, the initial evaluation was quite limited in scope, focusing predominantly on a single dataset and model architecture. While the proposed ZeroFL schemes show promising improvements over naive sparse FL, broader validation is necessary for full acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

**Comments after rebutal :** I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets (SpeechCommands and FEMNIST, as shown in Table 1 and Appendix A.4). The inclusion of these additional datasets significantly strengthens the empirical backing for ZeroFL's effectiveness. I have increased my score to reflect the same.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method (SWAT) and analyzes its limitations. Then, the method proposes three strategies to improve upon SWAT. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 dataset and show some improvements compared to SWAT.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has following strengths:
1.	Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important.
2.	The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT.
3.	Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting.

The paper has following weaknesses:
1.	The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses).
2.	One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here.

I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method (SWAT) and analyzes its limitations. Then, the method proposes three strategies to improve upon SWAT. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 dataset and show some improvements compared to SWAT.

Main Review:
The paper has following strengths:
1.	Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important.
2.	The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT.
3.	Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting.

The paper has following weaknesses:
1.	The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses).
2.	One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here.

Summary Of The Review:
Even though the reviewer is quite happy about the problem addressed in this paper, it lacks a solid experimental section. I would be willing to increase the score if the authors can present a more comprehensive results section.

Post rebuttal:
I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning, a critical challenge given the resource constraints of client devices in FL. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method, Sparse Weights and Activations Training (SWAT), and conducts a thorough analysis of its limitations when directly applied to the federated setting. This analysis, detailed in Section 4.3, highlights unique aspects such as the impact of periodic aggregation and client statefulness on sparsity patterns. Then, the method proposes ZeroFL, a novel framework comprising three distinct strategies (Top-K-Weights, Diff on Top-K-Weights, and Top-K-Weights Diff) to improve upon SWAT by leveraging local sparsification before uplink communication. Experiments are initially conducted on the CIFAR-10 dataset, and subsequently expanded to include SpeechCommands and FEMNIST, demonstrating notable improvements in accuracy and significant reductions in communication costs compared to the adapted SWAT baseline.

**Main Review:**
The paper has following strengths:

1.  Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important. The abstract clearly articulates that on-device training, which repeats hundreds of times and can involve thousands of devices, accounts for the majority of the time and totality of energy consumption at the client side. Addressing this bottleneck, especially on commodity devices like smartphones and IoT devices, is crucial for enabling the widespread adoption and scalability of FL for applications like next-word prediction or physical activity detection. The focus on accelerating the *training* phase, rather than just inference or communication, is a timely and impactful contribution to the field.

2.  The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT. ZeroFL introduces three distinct mechanisms—Top-K-Weights, Diff on Top-K-Weights, and Top-K-Weights Diff—each designed to mitigate the accuracy degradation observed when SWAT is naively applied to FL. These strategies, summarized in Algorithm 1, specifically target the "dilution of important information during the aggregation process" identified in Section 4.3. The experimental results, particularly those presented in Table 1 for CIFAR-10, SpeechCommands, and FEMNIST, consistently show that ZeroFL achieves better accuracy retention, with gains of up to +2.3% for 90% sparsity in non-IID SpeechCommands and +1.5% for 95% sparsity in CIFAR-10, while simultaneously offering substantial communication cost reductions (e.g., up to 7.4x savings for 95% sparsity).

3.  Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting. The detailed sparsification effect analysis in Section 4.3 provides valuable empirical observations that underpin the design of ZeroFL. Specifically, the findings that "the locations of zero-valued weights in the global model remain constant throughout most of the training rounds" and that a "significant overlap exists between clients across all CNN layers" (as illustrated compellingly in Figure 2 and Figure 3, including the larger versions in Appendix A.3) offer a foundational understanding of sparse training dynamics in FL. These insights—that a core set of important weights tends to be stable across clients and rounds—are pivotal for motivating the selective communication and aggregation strategies of ZeroFL, moving beyond a simple adaptation of centralized sparse training.

The paper has following weaknesses:

1.  The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses). The initial submission's reliance solely on CIFAR-10, while a common benchmark, limited the generalizability of the findings. Federated learning often deals with highly heterogeneous and diverse data distributions, making it imperative to test methods across a wider array of tasks, data types (e.g., text, more complex images), and client distributions. Benchmarks like LEAF offer pre-partitioned datasets designed to simulate real-world FL scenarios with varying levels of non-IIDness and client numbers, which would further stress-test the robustness of ZeroFL.

2.  One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here. While ZeroFL effectively addresses on-device computation and communication costs through sparsity, it operates within the standard FedAvg framework. Exploring how ZeroFL's sparsity techniques interact with or could be integrated into advanced FL optimization algorithms that primarily focus on accelerating *convergence rates* (e.g., reducing the number of communication rounds or local epochs) would provide a more holistic view of the overall training efficiency. Understanding this interplay could reveal new avenues for combining the benefits of both approaches—faster convergence and more efficient on-device processing—to achieve even greater practical impact.

**Summary Of The Review:**
Even though the reviewer is quite happy about the problem addressed in this paper, it initially lacked a solid experimental section. The problem of accelerating on-device training in federated learning is highly relevant and the analytical insights into sparse FL dynamics are commendable. I would be willing to increase the score if the authors can present a more comprehensive results section.

**Post rebuttal:**
I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. The addition of results for the SpeechCommands and FEMNIST datasets (as seen in Table 1 and Appendix A.4/A.5), along with the discussion of different non-IID settings (α=1.0 and α=1000), significantly enhances the empirical evaluation and addresses my concerns regarding experimental comprehensiveness. This expanded evaluation demonstrates the applicability and benefits of ZeroFL across a broader range of FL scenarios. I am satisfied that the authors have taken this feedback into account. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.
